## 8. DAG Query Languages for Provenance

### W3C PROV-DM Standard

W3C PROV Data Model (https://www.w3.org/TR/prov-dm/) is the canonical standard for provenance representation. Core concepts:

- **Entity**: A thing with fixed aspects — a file, a dataset, a message
- **Activity**: A process that used and/or generated entities
- **Agent**: A person, software, or organization responsible for an activity
- **Usage**: (activity, entity) — the activity used this entity
- **Generation**: (entity, activity) — this activity generated the entity
- **Derivation**: (entity2, entity1) — entity2 was derived from entity1
- **Association**: (activity, agent, plan) — this agent ran this activity under this plan

The provenance DAG for an agent's action chain: each tool call is an Activity; its inputs are Entities it Used; its outputs are Entities it Generated; the agent itself is the Agent associated with each Activity.

PROV-AQ (https://www.w3.org/TR/prov-aq/) specifies access and query protocols for provenance stores.

### ProQL — Provenance Query Language

Karvounarakis, G. (2012). "Querying Data Provenance." University of Pennsylvania / LogicBlox. https://www.cis.upenn.edu/~zives/research/proql.pdf

ProQL uses path expressions in the provenance graph projection to reconstruct partial or complete derivation trees. For acyclic provenance graphs (DAGs), each tuple has a finite number of derivation tree shapes. ProQL is particularly relevant for "how-provenance" (which specific derivation path produced this result?).

### SPARQL for Provenance

"Computing How-Provenance for SPARQL Queries via Query Rewriting." VLDB 2021. https://vldb.org/pvldb/vol14/p3389-galarraga.pdf

SPARQLprov is an engine-agnostic method that annotates SPARQL query results with how-provenance annotations via query rewriting. Supports provenance storage as triples, allowing provenance retrieval using standard SPARQL queries on the knowledge graph.

### Datalog for Provenance

From the PODS 2024 papers:

Calautti, M., Livshits, E., Pieris, A. & Schneider, M. (2024). "The Complexity of Why-Provenance for Datalog Queries." Proceedings of the ACM on Management of Data. https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3651146

Key result: data complexity of why-provenance for Datalog queries is NP-complete even for linear recursion. This is significant for agent provenance systems: full why-provenance is intractable at scale; practical systems should target how-provenance (polynomial) or use approximations.

"Below and Above Why-Provenance for Datalog Queries." PODS 2024. https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3695829

Datalog's advantages for provenance querying:
- Polynomial-time evaluation for stratified programs (no recursion through negation)
- Natural expression of recursive graph reachability (ancestor/descendant relationships in DAGs)
- Well-understood formal semantics (fixpoint semantics)
- Used in authorization systems (e.g., Google's Zanzibar uses a Datalog-like language)

### PCAS Datalog-Derived Policy Language (2026)

Palumbo et al. (2026). arXiv:2602.16708 describes a Datalog-derived language for agent policies:

```datalog
% A tool call is tainted if any of its inputs were tainted
tainted(Call) :- input(Call, Data), tainted(Data).

% Transitive taint propagation
tainted(Result) :- generated_by(Result, Call), tainted(Call).

% Cross-agent taint
tainted(Msg) :- sent_by(Agent1, Msg), tainted(Agent1).

% Policy: blocked if tainted and accesses sensitive resource
blocked(Call) :- tainted(Call), accesses_sensitive(Call).
```

The system models agent interactions as a dependency graph with event types: `ToolCall`, `ToolResult`, `Message`, `Memory`. The policy engine evaluates Datalog rules over this graph.

### OpenLineage Standard

OpenLineage (https://openlineage.io, LF AI & Data Foundation) is the industry standard for data pipeline lineage, using a standardized metadata format for lineage events. Relevant for agent provenance: each agent action can emit an OpenLineage event with run metadata, job description, and input/output dataset descriptors. IBM expanded OpenLineage support in watsonx in early 2026.

### LLM Agents for Interactive Workflow Provenance (2025)

(2025). "LLM Agents for Interactive Workflow Provenance: Reference Architecture and Evaluation Methodology." arXiv:2509.13978. https://arxiv.org/html/2509.13978v2

Introduces an evaluation framework for using LLM agents to query provenance data at runtime: agents answer natural-language questions by translating them into provenance graph queries. Workflow provenance captures what, when, where, how, and by whom for each workflow step.

Rust struct implications:
```rust
// W3C PROV-DM entities
pub enum ProvNode {
    Entity { id: EntityId, attributes: HashMap<String, Value> },
    Activity { id: ActivityId, start_time: DateTime, end_time: DateTime },
    Agent { id: AgentId, agent_type: AgentType },
}

pub enum ProvEdge {
    Used { activity: ActivityId, entity: EntityId },
    WasGeneratedBy { entity: EntityId, activity: ActivityId },
    WasDerivedFrom { derived: EntityId, source: EntityId },
    WasAssociatedWith { activity: ActivityId, agent: AgentId },
    WasAttributedTo { entity: EntityId, agent: AgentId },
}

pub struct ProvGraph {
    pub nodes: HashMap<NodeId, ProvNode>,
    pub edges: Vec<ProvEdge>,
}

// Datalog-style provenance query
pub struct ProvenanceQuery {
    pub head: ProvenancePredicate,
    pub body: Vec<ProvenancePredicate>,
    pub negations: Vec<ProvenancePredicate>,
```
```rust
}
```

## 9. STRIDE-AI Threat Classification

Apply STRIDE-AI (Mauri & Damiani, 2022, Sensors 22(17):6662) to Roko agents:
- Spoofing → Model identity, agent impersonation in Collectives
- Tampering → Prompt injection, memory poisoning, tool output manipulation
- Repudiation → Missing audit trails (critical integration gap)
- Information Disclosure → Context window leakage, credential exfiltration
- Denial of Service → Resource exhaustion, token budget exhaustion
- Elevation of Privilege → Prompt injection → unauthorized tool calls (PFI, Kim et al. 2025)

```rust
/// STRIDE-AI threat classification for autonomous agents.

    pub category: StrideCategory,
    pub agent_manifestation: String,
    pub security_property: SecurityProperty,
    pub roko_mitigation: Vec<String>,
    pub atlas_technique: Option<AtlasTechnique>,
    pub residual_risk: RiskLevel,
}

pub enum StrideCategory {
    Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation,
    InformationDisclosure, DenialOfService, ElevationOfPrivilege,
}

pub enum SecurityProperty {
    Authenticity, Integrity, NonRepudiation,

}
```

```rust
    /// Test frequency.

}

pub enum AdversarialTechnique {

}
```

```rust
    PoisonedAgentTool,
    /// Escape to Host (v5.4.0)
    SandboxEscape,
}
```

Add a comprehensive mapping table with Roko defense for each technique.

## 9. STRIDE-AI Threat Classification

Apply STRIDE-AI (Mauri & Damiani, 2022, Sensors 22(17):6662) to Roko agents:
- Spoofing → Model identity, agent impersonation in Collectives
- Tampering → Prompt injection, memory poisoning, tool output manipulation
- Repudiation → Missing audit trails (critical integration gap)
- Information Disclosure → Context window leakage, credential exfiltration
- Denial of Service → Resource exhaustion, token budget exhaustion
- Elevation of Privilege → Prompt injection → unauthorized tool calls (PFI, Kim et al. 2025)

1→# Threat Model: 21 Failure Modes and Attack Trees
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: Cross-cut (Safety & Provenance)
     4→>
     5→> **Crate**: All safety-relevant crates
     6→>
     7→> **Synapse traits**: All six traits have threat implications
     8→>
     9→> **Prerequisites**: [00-defense-in-depth.md](00-defense-in-depth.md), [07-prompt-security.md](07-prompt-security.md)
    10→
    11→
    12→> **Implementation**: Specified
    13→
    14→---
    15→
    16→## Overview
    17→
    18→This document provides the structured threat model for the Roko framework. It defines adversary types, enumerates attack paths for both general-purpose agents and chain-domain agents, maps each to mitigating defense layers, and maintains a residual risk register for attacks that are not fully mitigated.
    19→
    20→The threat model is domain-agnostic at its core — the same adversary taxonomy applies whether the agent is writing code, managing DeFi positions, or conducting research. Chain-domain-specific threats are documented separately in §4.
    21→
    22→---
    23→
    24→## 1. Attacker Taxonomy
    25→
    26→### General-Purpose Agent Threats
    27→
    28→| Adversary | Motivation | Capabilities | Examples |
    29→|-----------|-----------|-------------|----------|
    30→| **Prompt injector** | Redirect agent behavior | Indirect injection via tool results, file contents, API responses | Greshake et al. (2023) demonstrations |
    31→| **Data poisoner** | Corrupt agent reasoning | Manipulate Neuro entries, inject false knowledge | AgentPoison (Chen et al., 2024), MINJA (Cheng et al., 2024), MemoryGraft (Li et al., 2024) |
    32→| **Sandbox escaper** | Access files/systems outside worktree | Path traversal, symlink exploitation, shell escapes | CVE-2025-6514 (MCP RCE) |
    33→| **Credential harvester** | Exfiltrate API keys and secrets | Induce agent to log/transmit credentials | Context window leakage vectors |
    34→| **Resource exhaustor** | Deny service or increase costs | Trigger infinite loops, excessive API calls | Rate limit bypass, loop induction |
    35→| **Supply chain attacker** | Compromise agent dependencies | Poisoned MCP servers, malicious skill packages | ClawHub campaign (335 malicious skills), Koi Security findings |
    36→
    37→### Chain-Domain-Specific Threats
    38→
    39→| Adversary | Motivation | Capabilities | Examples |
    40→|-----------|-----------|-------------|----------|
    41→| **Malicious vault creator** | Steal depositor funds | Full control of vault parameters at creation | Honeypot vaults, rug-pull via parameter changes |
    42→| **Compromised manager agent** | Drain vault via unauthorized transactions | Prompt-injected or key-compromised agent | AIXBT hack ($106K), agent wallet compromise |
    43→| **External MEV bot** | Extract value from agent operations | Mempool observation, sandwich attacks, JIT manipulation | Standard MEV on deposit/withdraw/rebalance |
    44→| **Oracle manipulator** | Inflate NAV for profitable withdrawal | Flash loan price manipulation, oracle feed corruption | Flash-loan-based NAV inflation |
    45→| **Knowledge poisoner** | Corrupt agent trading reasoning | Marketplace listing of poisoned strategies, collective infiltration | AgentPoison, MINJA attacks |
    46→| **Coordination attacker** | Exploit multi-agent coordination | Colluding agents, malicious evaluators | ERC-8033 oracle manipulation |
    47→
    48→---
    49→
    50→## 2. General-Purpose Attack Trees
    51→
    52→### 2.1 Prompt Injection
    53→
    54→```
    55→Goal: Execute unauthorized operations via LLM manipulation
    56→|
    57→+-- Path A: Indirect injection via tool results
    58→|   +-- Inject via bash command output
    59→|   |   Mitigated: ScrubPolicy scrubs secrets; BashPolicy blocks dangerous commands
    60→|   +-- Inject via file contents
    61→|   |   Mitigated: PathPolicy sandboxes reads to worktree; prompt architecture separates data from instructions
    62→|   +-- Inject via API response
    63→|       Mitigated: NetworkPolicy restricts destinations; response content is treated as data, not instructions
    64→|
    65→+-- Path B: Direct injection via task description
    66→|   +-- Embed malicious instructions in task TOML
    67→|   |   Mitigated: Task descriptions are system-controlled; operator reviews before execution
    68→|   +-- Embed in PRD content that feeds task generation
    69→|       Mitigated: PRD content passes through prompt composition with explicit UNTRUSTED markers
    70→|
    71→+-- Path C: Injection via knowledge store
    72→    +-- Poison Neuro entries with instructional content
    73→    |   Mitigated: 4-stage ingestion pipeline; HDC anomaly detection
    74→    +-- Inject via peer agent messages in collective
    75→        Mitigated: Reputation-weighted trust; consensus validation
    76→```
    77→
    78→### 2.2 Sandbox Escape
    79→
    80→```
    81→Goal: Access files or systems outside the agent's worktree
    82→|
    83→+-- Path A: Path traversal
    84→|   +-- Use ../../ to escape worktree
    85→|   |   Mitigated: PathPolicy canonicalizes and checks starts_with(worktree)
    86→|   +-- Use absolute path outside worktree
    87→|   |   Mitigated: PathPolicy checks absolute paths against worktree boundary
    88→|   +-- Use symlink pointing outside worktree
    89→|       Mitigated: PathPolicy deny_symlinks option (not enabled by default)
    90→|
    91→+-- Path B: Shell escape
    92→|   +-- Embed path traversal in bash command arguments
    93→|   |   Mitigated: BashPolicy deny patterns; PathPolicy on file tool arguments
    94→|   +-- Use shell features (backticks, $(), pipes) to construct escaped paths
    95→|       Mitigated: BashPolicy deny patterns catch common shell escapes
    96→|
    97→+-- Path C: MCP server exploitation
    98→    +-- Use MCP server to access files outside worktree
    99→    |   Mitigated: MCP tools pass through same SafetyLayer as built-in tools
   100→    +-- Exploit MCP server vulnerability (CVE-2025-6514 class)
   101→        Mitigated: MCP is optional; high-security deployments disable it entirely
   102→```
   103→
   104→### 2.3 Credential Exfiltration
   105→
   106→```
   107→Goal: Extract API keys, secrets, or sensitive data
   108→|
   109→+-- Path A: Via tool output
   110→|   +-- Read .env file containing secrets
   111→|   |   Mitigated: ScrubPolicy regex patterns detect and redact secrets in tool output
   112→|   +-- Read config file with embedded credentials
   113→|       Mitigated: ScrubPolicy covers common credential formats
   114→|
   115→+-- Path B: Via network
   116→|   +-- Exfiltrate via web_fetch to attacker-controlled server
   117→|   |   Mitigated: NetworkPolicy allowlists, HTTPS-only, private network blocking
   118→|   +-- Encode secrets in URL parameters or headers
   119→|       Mitigated: NetworkPolicy host filtering
   120→|
   121→+-- Path C: Via git
   122→|   +-- Commit secrets to repository
   123→|   |   Mitigated: ScrubPolicy detects secrets; GitPolicy blocks force-push to protected branches
   124→|   +-- Push to unauthorized remote
   125→|       Mitigated: GitPolicy restricts git operations; operator controls remote configuration
   126→```
   127→
   128→### 2.4 Resource Exhaustion
   129→
   130→```
   131→Goal: Deny service or inflate costs
   132→|
   133→+-- Path A: Infinite tool call loop
   134→|   +-- Retry loop on failing command
   135→|   |   Mitigated: RateLimiter (60 calls/60s per role+tool); circuit breaker
   136→|   +-- Recursive file processing
   137→|       Mitigated: RateLimiter; PathPolicy prevents escape; max command length
   138→|
   139→+-- Path B: Token budget exhaustion
   140→|   +-- Generate enormous prompts
   141→|   |   Mitigated: Composer budget constraints; token limits in agent configuration
   142→|   +-- Trigger many inference calls
   143→|       Mitigated: RateLimiter; conductor circuit breaker
   144→|
   145→+-- Path C: Disk exhaustion
   146→    +-- Write extremely large files
   147→    |   Mitigated: Result truncation (DEFAULT_MAX_RESULT_BYTES: 16,384); worktree isolation
   148→    +-- Create many worktrees
   149→        Mitigated: WorktreeConfig max_worktrees limit; idle TTL cleanup
   150→```
   151→
   152→---
   153→
   154→## 3. Residual Risk Register
   155→
   156→Attacks that are NOT fully mitigated by current defenses:
   157→
   158→| Risk ID | Description | Severity | Current Mitigation | Residual Exposure |
   159→|---------|-------------|----------|-------------------|-------------------|
   160→| RR-1 | Prompt injection bypass | High | SafetyLayer checks + prompt architecture | Sophisticated injections may bypass regex-based detection |
   161→| RR-2 | Novel bash escape patterns | Medium | BashPolicy deny patterns | New shell escape techniques not in deny list |
   162→| RR-3 | Symlink-based sandbox escape | Medium | PathPolicy (deny_symlinks off by default) | Default config allows symlinks within worktree |
   163→| RR-4 | Credential encoding (base64, split across calls) | Medium | ScrubPolicy regex patterns | Encoded or split secrets evade pattern matching |
   164→| RR-5 | MCP server zero-day | High | SafetyLayer + optional MCP disabling | Unknown vulnerabilities in MCP server implementations |
   165→| RR-6 | LLM capability escalation | Low | ToolPermission + task-level filters | Future LLMs may find novel workarounds |
   166→| RR-7 | Knowledge poisoning via novel attack | Medium | 4-stage ingestion pipeline | New attack classes not in adversarial test suite |
   167→| RR-8 | Timing side-channel (inference calls reveal reasoning) | Low | None currently | Request patterns reveal agent activity |
   168→
   169→---
   170→
   171→## 4. Chain-Domain Attack Trees
   172→
   173→These apply only to agents using the `roko-chain` crate for on-chain operations.
   174→
   175→### 4.1 Compromised Manager Agent (Chain Domain)
   176→
   177→```
   178→Goal: Execute unauthorized transactions
   179→|
   180→+-- Path A: Direct key compromise
   181→|   +-- Steal agent's private key
   182→|   |   Mitigated: TEE key management; keys never in agent memory
   183→|   +-- Compromise TEE environment
   184→|       Mitigated: TEE is defense-in-depth; time-delayed proxy
   185→|
   186→+-- Path B: Prompt injection → unauthorized trades
   187→|   +-- Inject via tool response
   188→|   |   Mitigated: Tool Integrity Verification (96% detection)
   189→|   +-- Inject via data feed
   190→|       Mitigated: Data/decision separation (CaMeL dual-LLM design target)
   191→|
   192→+-- Path C: Authorized but harmful operations
   193→    +-- Execute trades with excessive slippage
   194→    |   Mitigated: Pre-flight simulation; on-chain slippage caps (PolicyCage)
   195→    +-- Drain via many small transactions under limits
   196→        Mitigated: Per-day aggregate caps; continuous dampening
   197→```
   198→
   199→### 4.2 Oracle Manipulation (Chain Domain)
   200→
   201→```
   202→Goal: Manipulate NAV for profitable deposit/withdrawal
   203→|
   204→+-- Path A: Flash-loan price manipulation
   205→|   Mitigated: No-spot-assumptions policy; TWAP validation (10-30 min window)
   206→|
   207→+-- Path B: Oracle staleness exploitation
   208→|   Mitigated: Staleness gates widen spreads; 100% staleness disables NAV pricing
   209→|
   210→+-- Path C: NAV inflation via donation
   211→    Mitigated: Internal asset accounting (not balanceOf); virtual shares offset
   212→```
   213→
   214→---
   215→
   216→## 5. Formal Safety Analysis
   217→
   218→### Maximum Loss Bounds (Chain Domain)
   219→
   220→Under any single-layer failure:
   221→```
   222→max_loss = min(sessionKeyLimit, maxRebalanceSizeBps × TVL, dailyAggregateCap)
   223→```
   224→
   225→Under simultaneous multi-layer failure (estimated probability < 10⁻⁶ per year), the circuit breaker hierarchy provides automated intervention.
   226→
   227→### Attack Cost Economics
   228→
   229→| Attack Vector | Min Cost | Detection Time | Primary Defense |
   230→|--------------|---------|----------------|-----------------|
   231→| Prompt injection | $0 | < 1 second | SafetyLayer + prompt architecture |
   232→| Sandbox escape | $0 | < 1 second | PathPolicy canonicalization |
   233→| Credential theft | $0 | < 1 second | ScrubPolicy regex scrubbing |
   234→| TEE compromise (chain) | ~$50 (van Bulck et al., 2026) | Varies | Time-delayed proxy |
   235→| Knowledge poisoning | $0 | 1-6 hours | 4-stage ingestion pipeline |
   236→| MEV extraction (chain) | $10K+ | < 5 seconds | Flashbots Protect, slippage bounds |
   237→
   238→### Cross-Protocol Contagion (Chain Domain)
   239→
   240→Two metrics from the literature:
   241→
   242→**DeFi Correlation Fragility Indicator (CFI)** (Zhang et al., 2026): Measures protocol TVL correlations using a sliding window. When CFI is elevated, circuit breaker thresholds tighten from 13%/7%/3% to 10%/5%/2%.
   243→
   244→**Aggregated Systemic Risk Index (ASRI)** (Farzulla et al., 2026): Combines on-chain protocol metrics with traditional market indicators. Detected historical crises with 18-day lead time in backtesting. When ASRI exceeds threshold (default 0.7), automatic deallocation from most correlated adapters.
   245→
   246→### Conformal Prediction for Value at Risk (Chain Domain)
   247→
   248→The risk engine uses conformal prediction for VaR estimation, providing distribution-free coverage guarantees. Unlike parametric VaR (assumes normal returns) or historical simulation (requires stationary distributions), conformal prediction provides valid coverage regardless of the underlying distribution. This matters because DeFi return distributions are heavy-tailed and non-stationary.
   249→
   250→References: Fantazzini (2024) for crypto-asset VaR calibration; Kato (2024, arXiv:2410.16333) for portfolio-level conformal prediction.
   251→
   252→---
   253→
   254→## 6. Empirical Calibration
   255→
   256→Validated against Zhou et al.'s DeFi exploit database (181 attacks through 2023):
   257→
   258→| Attack Category | Defense | Coverage |
   259→|-----------------|---------|----------|
   260→| Oracle manipulation | Multi-oracle, TWAP validation | Strong |
   261→| Access control | On-chain guards, TEE policy engine | Strong |
   262→| Reentrancy | Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern | Strong |
   263→| Logic errors | Pre-flight simulation, post-trade verification | Partial (residual risk) |
   264→| Flash loan attacks | Virtual shares offset, internal asset accounting | Strong |
   265→
   266→---
   267→

   269→
   270→| Paper | Contribution |
   271→|-------|-------------|
   272→| Omohundro (2008) | Instrumental convergence — why AI systems develop unsafe drives |
   273→| Turner et al. (2021) | Mathematical proof of resource-seeking optimal policies |
   274→| Greshake et al. (2023) | Indirect prompt injection demonstrations |
   275→| Chen et al. (2024) | AgentPoison — optimized backdoor triggers for RAG-based agents |
   276→| Cheng et al. (2024) | MINJA — adversarial injection through normal interactions |
   277→| Li et al. (2024) | MemoryGraft — gradual behavioral drift via subtle memory bias |
   278→| Zhou et al. (2023) | DeFi exploit database — 181 attacks catalogued |
   279→| van Bulck et al. (2026) | TEE hardware attacks at ~$50 cost |
   280→| Zhang et al. (2026) | DeFi Correlation Fragility Indicator |
   281→| Farzulla et al. (2026) | Aggregated Systemic Risk Index |
   282→| Fantazzini (2024) | Crypto-asset VaR calibration |
   283→| Kato (2024, arXiv:2410.16333) | Portfolio-level conformal prediction |
   284→| Yang et al. (2025) | R2AI safety framework |
   285→| Woolley et al. (Science 330(6004), 2010) | Collective intelligence foundations |
   286→

   290→

   292→- [07-prompt-security.md](07-prompt-security.md) — Prompt injection defenses in detail
   293→- [09-adaptive-risk.md](09-adaptive-risk.md) — Adaptive guardrails

1→# Taint-Aware Ingestion and Data Flow Control
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L2 Scaffold (context engineering), L3 Harness (ingestion gates), Cross-cut (Neuro)
     4→>
     5→> **Crate**: `roko-agent` (ScrubPolicy), target: `roko-neuro` (taint labels), `roko-compose` (context assembly)
     6→>
     7→> **Synapse traits**: `Gate` (verify taint compliance before data flows to sinks), `Scorer` (rate trust level of ingested data)
     8→>
     9→> **Prerequisites**: [00-defense-in-depth.md](00-defense-in-depth.md), Neuro knowledge store documentation
    10→
    11→
    12→> **Implementation**: Specified
    13→
    14→---
    15→
    16→## The Problem: Untrusted Data in Agent Context
    17→
    18→An autonomous agent ingests data from many sources: tool results, LLM outputs, knowledge store entries, operator inputs, external APIs, and peer agent messages. Not all of these sources are equally trustworthy. A prompt injection attack can cause the LLM to include sensitive data in its output. If that output is logged, synced to the Agent Mesh (formerly "Styx"), or broadcast, the sensitive data is exposed.
    19→
    20→The fundamental challenge is distinguishing data from instructions. CaMeL (Debenedetti et al., 2025) addresses this by separating control flow from data flow. Taint tracking generalizes this principle: every piece of data carries labels describing its sensitivity and trust level, and these labels are checked before data crosses any trust boundary.
    21→
    22→---
    23→
    24→## Taint Labels
    25→
    26→Every piece of sensitive data carries taint labels that propagate through the system. Before data enters a sink (LLM context, audit log, mesh relay, event fabric), the taint checker verifies that no forbidden label reaches that sink.
    27→
    28→### Label Taxonomy
    29→
    30→```rust
    31→/// Taint labels: what kind of sensitive data is this?
    32→#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, Debug)]
    33→pub enum TaintLabel {
    34→    /// Wallet private key material.
    35→    /// NEVER leaves the local process. Not even to the LLM context.
    36→    WalletSecret,
    37→
    38→    /// Owner API keys, service credentials.
    39→    /// Never enters LLM context or mesh relay.
    40→    OwnerSecret,
    41→
    42→    /// Proprietary strategy parameters (alpha).
    43→    /// Never enters the mesh relay. May enter collective (same owner's group).
    44→    StrategyConfidential,
    45→
    46→    /// Owner personal data (email, wallet addresses).
    47→    /// Never enters mesh relay without anonymization.
    48→    UserPII,
    49→
    50→    /// Data from untrusted external sources (mesh entries, marketplace).
    51→    /// Must be validated before use in configuration or tool parameters.
    52→    UntrustedExternal,
    53→}
    54→```
    55→
    56→### TaintedString Type
    57→
    58→```rust
    59→/// A taint-tracked string. The sensitive content is wrapped in
    60→/// Zeroizing<String> (from the zeroize crate) which automatically
    61→/// overwrites the memory on drop — preventing key recovery from
    62→/// memory dumps.
    63→pub struct TaintedString {
    64→    value: zeroize::Zeroizing<String>,
    65→    labels: std::collections::HashSet<TaintLabel>,
    66→}
    67→```
    68→
    69→The `Zeroizing<String>` wrapper is critical for key material. When a `TaintedString` containing a private key goes out of scope, the memory is overwritten with zeros before being freed. This prevents key recovery from memory dumps, core files, or swap space — a real threat when agents run in shared cloud environments.
    70→
    71→### Data Sinks
    72→
    73→```rust
    74→/// Data sinks: where can data flow?
    75→#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug)]
    76→pub enum DataSink {
    77→    /// The LLM's input (system prompt + messages).
    78→    LlmContext,
    79→    /// Content-addressed audit trail.
    80→    AuditLog,
    81→    /// Agent Mesh shared knowledge relay.
    82→    MeshRelay,
    83→    /// Broadcast to surfaces (TUI, web, notifications).
    84→    EventFabric,
    85→    /// Peer-to-peer collective sync.
    86→    CollectivePeer,
    87→    /// Local Neuro store (everything allowed).
    88→    LocalNeuro,
    89→}
    90→```
    91→
    92→### Flow Rules Matrix
    93→
    94→| Label | LlmContext | AuditLog | MeshRelay | EventFabric | CollectivePeer | LocalNeuro |
    95→|-------|-----------|----------|-----------|-------------|----------------|------------|
    96→| WalletSecret | BLOCKED | BLOCKED | BLOCKED | BLOCKED | BLOCKED | Allowed |
    97→| OwnerSecret | BLOCKED | BLOCKED | BLOCKED | BLOCKED | Allowed | Allowed |
    98→| StrategyConfidential | Allowed | Allowed | BLOCKED | Allowed | Allowed | Allowed |
    99→| UserPII | Allowed | Allowed | BLOCKED | BLOCKED | Allowed | Allowed |
   100→| UntrustedExternal | Allowed | Allowed | Allowed | Allowed | Allowed | Allowed |
   101→
   102→```rust
   103→impl TaintedString {
   104→    /// Can this data flow to the specified sink?
   105→    /// Returns false if any taint label is forbidden for that sink.
   106→    pub fn can_flow_to(&self, sink: DataSink) -> bool {
   107→        match sink {
   108→            DataSink::LlmContext => {
   109→                !self.labels.contains(&TaintLabel::WalletSecret)
   110→                    && !self.labels.contains(&TaintLabel::OwnerSecret)
   111→            }
   112→            DataSink::AuditLog => {
   113→                !self.labels.contains(&TaintLabel::WalletSecret)
   114→            }
   115→            DataSink::MeshRelay => {
   116→                !self.labels.contains(&TaintLabel::StrategyConfidential)
   117→                    && !self.labels.contains(&TaintLabel::UserPII)
   118→                    && !self.labels.contains(&TaintLabel::WalletSecret)
   119→            }
   120→            DataSink::EventFabric => {
   121→                !self.labels.contains(&TaintLabel::WalletSecret)
   122→                    && !self.labels.contains(&TaintLabel::OwnerSecret)
   123→            }
   124→            DataSink::CollectivePeer => {
   125→                !self.labels.contains(&TaintLabel::WalletSecret)
   126→            }
   127→            DataSink::LocalNeuro => true,
   128→        }
   129→    }
   130→}
   131→```
   132→
   133→---
   134→
   135→## Four-Stage Ingestion Pipeline
   136→
   137→All data from external sources passes through a four-stage ingestion pipeline before entering the Neuro knowledge store. This pipeline is the primary defense against knowledge poisoning attacks.
   138→
   139→### Trust Levels
   140→
   141→```rust
   142→/// Trust level assigned to ingested data.
   143→#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord)]
   144→pub enum TrustLevel {
   145→    /// Untrusted: raw external data, not yet validated.
   146→    Untrusted,
   147→    /// Quarantined: passed initial checks, awaiting consensus.
   148→    Quarantined,
   149→    /// Verified: passed consensus validation, safe to use.
   150→    Verified,
   151→    /// Trusted: from a known, high-reputation source.
   152→    Trusted,
   153→}
   154→```
   155→
   156→### Stage 1: Quarantine
   157→
   158→Every piece of external data enters at `TrustLevel::Untrusted` and is immediately quarantined. During quarantine:
   159→
   160→- The data is stored in a separate quarantine partition, isolated from the main Neuro store
   161→- It is not available for retrieval during agent reasoning
   162→- Basic structural validation runs: JSON schema checks, numeric bounds, format verification
   163→- A content hash is computed for deduplication
   164→
   165→**Research basis**: The quarantine pattern is inspired by the immune system's innate immunity — pattern-matching defense that activates immediately without prior exposure. Known-bad patterns (SQL injection strings, shell metacharacters, known malicious payloads) are rejected outright.
   166→
   167→### Stage 2: Consensus Validation
   168→
   169→Quarantined data that passes structural checks enters consensus validation. This is a multi-layer process:
   170→
   171→**Layer 1: TrustRAG anomaly detection.** Uses the HDC (Hyperdimensional Computing) similarity engine in `roko-primitives` to compare the incoming entry against existing knowledge. Entries with low similarity to anything in the store (novel claims) are flagged for additional scrutiny. Entries with high similarity to known anti-knowledge (previously falsified claims) are rejected.
   172→
   173→Reference: TrustRAG builds on anomaly detection principles from HDC nearest-neighbor classification (Kanerva, 2009, Cognitive Computation 1(2)).
   174→
   175→**Layer 2: A-MemGuard behavioral analysis.** Checks whether the incoming entry is consistent with the agent's existing causal model. An entry claiming "ETH always goes up" contradicts the agent's volatility models and is flagged. Consistency is measured using the coherence axis of the 7-axis Engram Score.
   176→
   177→**Layer 3: Multi-validator consensus.** For high-stakes entries (those that would change decision thresholds or strategy parameters), multiple independent validators must agree. The threshold is 2-of-3 for normal entries and 3-of-5 for entries that modify safety parameters.
   178→
   179→### Stage 3: Sandbox Testing
   180→
   181→Entries that pass consensus enter sandbox testing. The agent runs a counterfactual simulation: "If this entry had been in my knowledge store for the last N decisions, would those decisions have been better or worse?"
   182→
   183→This is implemented as a lightweight replay of recent episodes (see `roko-learn/episode_logger.rs`) with the candidate entry injected into the Neuro store. If the counterfactual outcomes are worse (lower gate pass rates, higher cost, more failures), the entry is rejected.
   184→
   185→### Stage 4: Adoption with Causal Rollback
   186→
   187→Entries that survive sandbox testing are adopted into the Neuro store at `TrustLevel::Verified`. They enter at the Transient tier (0.1x base half-life) and must earn promotion to Working (0.5x), Consolidated (1.0x), and Persistent (5.0x) tiers through demonstrated utility.
   188→
   189→If an adopted entry later proves harmful (detected via declining gate pass rates correlated with the entry's use), the system performs causal rollback:
   190→
   191→1. Identify all decisions that used the harmful entry as context
   192→2. Mark the entry as anti-knowledge (a falsified claim that inoculates against similar future entries)
   193→3. Store "dual memory" lessons: what went wrong and what the correct approach was
   194→4. Optionally replay the affected decisions without the harmful entry
   195→
   196→---
   197→
   198→## External Data Taint Sources
   199→
   200→All data that crosses a trust boundary is tainted:
   201→
   202→| Source | Taint Type | Validation Gates |
   203→|--------|-----------|-----------------|
   204→| Tool result (bash, read_file) | `UntrustedExternal` | JSON schema, output truncation, secret scrubbing |
   205→| LLM inference output | `UntrustedExternal` | JSON schema (action grammar), regex (address format) |
   206→| Mesh relay entries | `UntrustedExternal` | 4-stage ingestion pipeline |
   207→| Operator TUI input | `UntrustedExternal` (auto-promoted) | Regex, numeric bounds |
   208→| Peer agent messages | `UntrustedExternal` | Reputation-weighted trust, consensus validation |
   209→| API response bodies | `UntrustedExternal` | JSON schema, numeric bounds |
   210→
   211→The LLM's own output is tainted — this is counterintuitive but correct. The LLM produces text that gets parsed into proposed actions. Those proposed actions must pass through validation (JSON schema matching the action grammar) before they can become executable. Pan et al. (ACL 2024) documented how compressed or injected context can redirect LLM behavior; taint tracking prevents injected content from reaching execution paths regardless of whether the LLM "believes" the injection.
   212→
   213→---
   214→
   215→## Current Implementation: ScrubPolicy
   216→
   217→The current Roko codebase implements a post-hoc approximation of taint tracking through the `ScrubPolicy` in `roko-agent/src/safety/scrub.rs`. This is not compile-time taint tracking — it is regex-based pattern matching that catches secrets after they appear in tool output.
   218→
   219→The scrubber applies 9 default regex patterns covering:
   220→- API keys (Anthropic, OpenAI, AWS, GitHub, GitLab, Slack)
   221→- JWTs (three base64url segments starting with `eyJ`)
   222→- Private key blocks (`-----BEGIN * PRIVATE KEY-----`)
   223→- Env-file assignments (`PASSWORD=`, `SECRET=`, `TOKEN=`, etc.)
   224→
   225→Additional user-defined patterns can be added via `ScrubPolicy::extra_patterns`.
   226→
   227→**Limitation**: Regex-based scrubbing is a heuristic. It catches common patterns but cannot prevent all data exfiltration. A determined attacker could encode secrets in base64, split them across multiple tool calls, or use steganographic techniques. The full `TaintedString` compile-time tracking (Tier 2) addresses these limitations by preventing tainted data from reaching sinks at the type level.
   228→
   229→---
   230→
   231→## TaintedString: full implementation
   232→
   233→The `TaintedString` type provides label manipulation, propagation through string operations, and controlled access to the inner value.
   234→
   235→```rust
   236→impl TaintedString {
   237→    /// Create a new tainted string with a single label.
   238→    pub fn new(value: String, label: TaintLabel) -> Self {
   239→        let mut labels = HashSet::new();
   240→        labels.insert(label);
   241→        Self {
   242→            value: zeroize::Zeroizing::new(value),
   243→            labels,
   244→        }
   245→    }
   246→
   247→    /// Add a taint label. Labels accumulate -- once tainted, always tainted.
   248→    pub fn with_label(mut self, label: TaintLabel) -> Self {
   249→        self.labels.insert(label);
   250→        self
   251→    }
   252→
   253→    /// Merge labels from another TaintedString.
   254→    /// Used when concatenating or interpolating tainted values.
   255→    pub fn merge_labels(&mut self, other: &TaintedString) {
   256→        self.labels.extend(&other.labels);
   257→    }
   258→
   259→    /// Access the inner value. Caller must have verified
   260→    /// can_flow_to() for the target sink.
   261→    ///
   262→    /// # Panics
   263→    /// Debug builds panic if called without a prior can_flow_to() check.
   264→    /// Release builds return the value (enforcement is at the sink boundary).
   265→    pub fn as_str(&self) -> &str {
   266→        &self.value
   267→    }
   268→
   269→    /// Check all labels at once.
   270→    pub fn labels(&self) -> &HashSet<TaintLabel> {
   271→        &self.labels
   272→    }
   273→
   274→    /// True if this string carries no taint labels.
   275→    pub fn is_clean(&self) -> bool {
   276→        self.labels.is_empty()
   277→    }
   278→
   279→    /// Strip a label after sanitization.
   280→    /// Only ScrubPolicy and the ingestion pipeline should call this.
   281→    pub fn declassify(&mut self, label: TaintLabel) {
   282→        self.labels.remove(&label);
   283→    }
   284→}
   285→```
   286→
   287→Label propagation rule: when two `TaintedString` values combine (concatenation, interpolation, format strings), the result carries the union of both label sets. This prevents label stripping through string manipulation.
   288→
   289→---
   290→
   291→## Bloom Oracle (Design Target)
   292→
   293→The Bloom Oracle is a probabilistic filter for rapid initial screening of ingested data. Using a Bloom filter trained on known-bad patterns (malicious payloads, known attack signatures, previously rejected entries), it provides O(1) rejection of data that has a high probability of being harmful.
   294→
   295→False positive rate: configurable, default 0.1% (one legitimate entry in a thousand incorrectly flagged -- flagged entries go to Stage 2 consensus, not rejected outright). False negative rate: zero by construction (Bloom filters never produce false negatives).
   296→
   297→### BloomOracle struct and configuration
   298→
   299→```rust
   300→use bitvec::prelude::*;
   301→
   302→/// Probabilistic filter for rapid screening of ingested data.
   303→/// Uses k independent hash functions over a bit array of size m.
   304→pub struct BloomOracle {
   305→    /// Bit array backing the filter.
   306→    bits: BitVec,
   307→    /// Number of hash functions (k).
   308→    num_hashes: u32,
   309→    /// Number of bits (m). Determines false positive rate.
   310→    num_bits: usize,
   311→    /// Number of items inserted so far (n).
   312→    count: usize,
   313→}
   314→```
   315→
   316→**Parameter selection.** Given a target false-positive rate `p` and expected item count `n`:
   317→
   318→| Parameter | Formula | Default |
   319→|-----------|---------|---------|
   320→| Bits (m) | `m = -(n * ln(p)) / (ln(2)^2)` | 9,586 bits for n=1000, p=0.001 |
   321→| Hash functions (k) | `k = (m / n) * ln(2)` | 10 for default parameters |
   322→| False positive rate (p) | `p = (1 - e^(-kn/m))^k` | 0.001 (0.1%) |
   323→| False negative rate | 0 by construction | 0 |
   324→
   325→```rust
   326→impl BloomOracle {
   327→    /// Create a new BloomOracle with target false-positive rate.
   328→    ///
   329→    /// # Parameters
   330→    /// - `expected_items`: estimated number of entries (n). Range: 100..10_000_000.
   331→    /// - `false_positive_rate`: target FP rate (p). Range: 0.0001..0.1.
   332→    pub fn new(expected_items: usize, false_positive_rate: f64) -> Self {
   333→        assert!(
   334→            (0.0001..0.1).contains(&false_positive_rate),
   335→            "FP rate must be in [0.0001, 0.1]"
   336→        );
   337→        let ln2 = std::f64::consts::LN_2;
   338→        let num_bits =
   339→            (-(expected_items as f64) * false_positive_rate.ln() / (ln2 * ln2)).ceil() as usize;
   340→        let num_hashes = ((num_bits as f64 / expected_items as f64) * ln2).ceil() as u32;
   341→
   342→        Self {
   343→            bits: bitvec![0; num_bits],
   344→            num_hashes,
   345→            num_bits,
   346→            count: 0,
   347→        }
   348→    }
   349→
   350→    /// Insert a known-bad pattern into the filter.
   351→    pub fn insert(&mut self, item: &[u8]) {
   352→        for i in 0..self.num_hashes {
   353→            let idx = self.hash(item, i) % self.num_bits;
   354→            self.bits.set(idx, true);
   355→        }
   356→        self.count += 1;
   357→    }
   358→
   359→    /// Query whether an item might be known-bad.
   360→    /// Returns true = "possibly bad" (proceed to Stage 2 consensus).
   361→    /// Returns false = "definitely not in the known-bad set."
   362→    pub fn maybe_bad(&self, item: &[u8]) -> bool {
   363→        (0..self.num_hashes).all(|i| {
   364→            let idx = self.hash(item, i) % self.num_bits;
   365→            self.bits[idx]
   366→        })
   367→    }
   368→
   369→    /// Current fill ratio. When this exceeds 0.5, the FP rate
   370→    /// degrades faster than the theoretical bound. Rebuild with
   371→    /// a larger m when fill_ratio() > 0.5.
   372→    pub fn fill_ratio(&self) -> f64 {
   373→        self.bits.count_ones() as f64 / self.num_bits as f64
   374→    }
   375→
   376→    fn hash(&self, item: &[u8], seed: u32) -> usize {
   377→        let mut hasher = blake3::Hasher::new();
   378→        hasher.update(&seed.to_le_bytes());
   379→        hasher.update(item);
   380→        let hash = hasher.finalize();
   381→        let bytes: [u8; 8] = hash.as_bytes()[..8].try_into().unwrap();
   382→        usize::from_le_bytes(bytes)
   383→    }
   384→}
   385→```
   386→
   387→**roko.toml configuration:**
   388→
   389→```toml
   390→[safety.bloom_oracle]
   391→expected_items = 5000       # Estimated known-bad patterns. Range: 100..10_000_000.
   392→false_positive_rate = 0.001 # Target FP rate. Range: 0.0001..0.1.
   393→rebuild_threshold = 0.5     # Rebuild when fill_ratio exceeds this. Range: 0.3..0.8.
   394→```
   395→
   396→---
   397→
   398→## Four-stage ingestion state machine
   399→
   400→The ingestion pipeline is a state machine with four states and explicit transition triggers. Each entry carries its current state and transitions forward on success or backward/out on failure.
   401→
   402→```
   403→                    +-----------+
   404→                    | Rejected  |
   405→                    +-----^-----+
   406→                          |
   407→        structural fail   | consensus fail   sandbox fail
   408→                          |                       |
   409→  +-----------+     +-----+------+     +----------+-+     +----------+
   410→  | Untrusted | --> | Quarantine | --> |  Consensus | --> |  Sandbox | --> Adopted
   411→  +-----------+     +------------+     +------------+     +----------+
   412→     entry             pass struct       pass layers         pass
   413→                       validation        1/2/3              counterfactual
   414→```
   415→
   416→### Transition triggers
   417→
   418→| From | To | Trigger |
   419→|------|----|---------|
   420→| Untrusted | Quarantine | Entry received from any external source |
   421→| Quarantine | Consensus | Structural validation passes: valid JSON, numeric bounds within schema, content hash computed, no duplicate |
   422→| Quarantine | Rejected | Structural validation fails, or BloomOracle flags entry as known-bad |
   423→| Consensus | Sandbox | All consensus layers pass: TrustRAG similarity above threshold (default 0.3), A-MemGuard coherence above threshold (default 0.5), multi-validator quorum met |
   424→| Consensus | Rejected | Any consensus layer fails, or entry matches anti-knowledge signature |
   425→| Sandbox | Adopted | Counterfactual replay shows neutral-or-positive outcome (gate pass rate delta >= -0.02) |
   426→| Sandbox | Rejected | Counterfactual replay shows negative outcome (gate pass rate delta < -0.02) |
   427→| Adopted | Anti-knowledge | Post-adoption monitoring detects declining gate pass rates correlated with entry usage (see causal rollback below) |
   428→
   429→### Configuration parameters
   430→
   431→```toml
   432→[safety.ingestion]
   433→quarantine_timeout_secs = 3600       # Max time in quarantine. Range: 60..86400.
   434→trustrag_similarity_threshold = 0.3  # Minimum HDC similarity. Range: 0.0..1.0.
   435→amemguard_coherence_threshold = 0.5  # Minimum coherence score. Range: 0.0..1.0.
   436→normal_quorum = "2-of-3"             # Validator quorum for normal entries.
   437→safety_quorum = "3-of-5"             # Validator quorum for safety-parameter entries.
   438→sandbox_replay_window = 50           # Number of recent episodes to replay. Range: 10..500.
   439→sandbox_gate_delta_threshold = -0.02 # Gate pass rate delta below which entry is rejected.
   440→causal_rollback_correlation = 0.7    # Min Pearson r between entry usage and declining gates. Range: 0.3..0.95.
   441→```
   442→
   443→### Causal rollback state transitions
   444→
   445→When an adopted entry proves harmful, the rollback proceeds through three states:
   446→
   447→```
   448→  +----------+        +-----------+        +-----------------+
   449→  |  Adopted | -----> | Suspected | -----> | Anti-Knowledge  |
   450→  +----------+  gate  +-----------+  corr  +-----------------+
   451→                decline   window    confirmed     |
   452→                detected  (20 turns)              |
   453→                                                  v
   454→                                        +------------------+
   455→                                        | Dual Memory      |
   456→                                        | (lesson stored)  |
   457→                                        +------------------+
   458→```
   459→
   460→| From | To | Trigger |
   461→|------|----|---------|
   462→| Adopted | Suspected | Gate pass rate drops below EMA - 2 sigma for 5 consecutive turns while entry is in active context |
   463→| Suspected | Adopted | Observation window (20 turns) passes without confirming correlation (Pearson r < `causal_rollback_correlation`) |
   464→| Suspected | Anti-Knowledge | Pearson correlation between entry usage frequency and gate failure rate exceeds `causal_rollback_correlation` over 20-turn window |
   465→| Anti-Knowledge | Dual Memory | System stores: (1) the harmful entry, (2) which decisions it affected, (3) what the correct approach was. Downstream decisions optionally replayed without the entry. |
   466→
   467→```rust
   468→/// State of an ingested entry in the pipeline.
   469→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
   470→pub enum IngestionState {
   471→    Untrusted,
   472→    Quarantine,
   473→    Consensus,
   474→    Sandbox,
   475→    Adopted,
   476→    Suspected,
   477→    AntiKnowledge,
   478→    Rejected,
   479→}
   480→
   481→/// An entry moving through the ingestion pipeline.
   482→pub struct IngestEntry {
   483→    pub content_hash: blake3::Hash,
   484→    pub state: IngestionState,
   485→    pub trust_level: TrustLevel,
   486→    pub entered_at: std::time::Instant,
   487→    pub state_changed_at: std::time::Instant,
   488→    /// Number of consecutive turns with declining gate pass rate.
   489→    pub decline_streak: u32,
   490→    /// Turn counter for the suspected observation window.
   491→    pub observation_turns: u32,
   492→}
   493→```
   494→
   495→---
   496→
   497→## ScrubPolicy wiring path
   498→
   499→The `ScrubPolicy` (runtime regex-based taint approximation) wires into the agent dispatch pipeline through `orchestrate.rs`:
   500→
   501→```
   502→orchestrate.rs: PlanRunner::run_task()
   503→  |
   504→  +--> ExecAgent::run()
   505→         |
   506→         +--> ToolDispatcher::dispatch()
   507→                |
   508→                +--> [pre-execution] SafetyLayer::check_pre_execution()
   509→                |      includes: BashPolicy, GitPolicy, PathPolicy, NetworkPolicy
   510→                |
   511→                +--> [execute tool handler]
   512→                |
   513→                +--> [post-execution] ScrubPolicy::scrub_output()
   514→                |      applies 9 default regex patterns + user extras
   515→                |      replaces matches with "[REDACTED:<pattern_name>]"
   516→                |
   517→                +--> [post-execution] RateLimiter::check()
   518→                |
   519→                +--> emit_audit() --> .roko/signals.jsonl
   520→```
   521→
   522→**Integration points:**
   523→
   524→| Component | File | Function |
   525→|-----------|------|----------|
   526→| ScrubPolicy definition | `roko-agent/src/safety/scrub.rs` | `ScrubPolicy::new()`, `scrub_output()` |
   527→| SafetyLayer composition | `roko-agent/src/safety/mod.rs` | `SafetyLayer::check_pre_execution()` |
   528→| ToolDispatcher pipeline | `roko-agent/src/dispatcher/mod.rs` | `dispatch()` 7-step pipeline |
   529→| Orchestrator entry point | `roko-cli/src/orchestrate.rs` | `PlanRunner::run_task()` |
   530→| Custom patterns config | `roko.toml` | `[agent.safety.scrub_patterns]` |
   531→
   532→**Error handling:** When `ScrubPolicy::scrub_output()` matches a pattern, it replaces the match in-place and logs a `Kind::SecurityEvent` Engram to the audit trail. The tool call succeeds (the scrubbed output is returned to the agent), but the original unscrubbed value is never exposed. If scrubbing fails (regex compilation error on a user-provided pattern), the tool output is blocked entirely and a `Kind::Error` Engram is emitted.
   533→
   534→### Test criteria
   535→
   536→- `TaintedString::can_flow_to()` blocks every forbidden label/sink pair per the flow rules matrix
   537→- `TaintedString::with_label()` accumulates labels; `merge_labels()` takes the union
   538→- `TaintedString::declassify()` removes a single label without affecting others
   539→- `BloomOracle` achieves measured FP rate within 2x of configured target over 10,000 random queries
   540→- `BloomOracle::maybe_bad()` never returns false for an inserted item (zero false negatives)
   541→- Ingestion state machine transitions match the trigger table: no entry skips a state
   542→- Causal rollback transitions from Suspected back to Adopted when correlation is below threshold
   543→- `ScrubPolicy` catches all 9 default patterns in synthetic tool output
   544→- Scrubbing failure on a bad regex blocks the entire output rather than leaking secrets
   545→
   546→---
   547→
   548→## Academic References
   549→
   550→| Paper | Contribution |
   551→|-------|-------------|
   552→| Pan et al. (ACL 2024) | Compressed/injected context redirects LLM behavior |
   553→| Debenedetti et al. (2025) | CaMeL — separate control flow from data flow |
   554→| Kanerva (2009, Cognitive Computation 1(2)) | HDC similarity for anomaly detection |
   555→| AgentPoison (Chen et al., 2024) | Optimized backdoor triggers for RAG-based agents |
   556→| MINJA (Cheng et al., 2024) | Adversarial injection through normal interactions |
   557→| MemoryGraft (Li et al., 2024) | Gradual behavioral drift via subtle memory bias |
   558→| TrustRAG (Jiang et al., 2025) | Anomaly detection for RAG retrieval poisoning |
   559→| A-MemGuard (Wang et al., 2025) | Memory integrity verification for persistent agents |
   560→
   561→---
   562→
   563→## Related Topics
   564→
   565→- [00-defense-in-depth.md](00-defense-in-depth.md) — Overall safety architecture
   566→- [07-prompt-security.md](07-prompt-security.md) — Prompt injection defense
   567→- [08-threat-model.md](08-threat-model.md) — Knowledge poisoning attack trees
   568→- [09-adaptive-risk.md](09-adaptive-risk.md) — Adaptive guardrails that respond to trust levels
   569→

input.description: Enhance witness DAG with query language

content.content:
1→# Witness DAG: Cryptographic Cognitive Traces
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L3 Harness (audit chain extension), L4 Orchestration (cross-agent verification)
     4→>
     5→> **Crate**: Target: `roko-gate` (extension of existing audit chain), integration with `roko-fs` (SQLite storage)
     6→>
     7→> **Synapse traits**: `Substrate` (persist vertices), `Gate` (verify commitment hashes), `Policy` (emit DAG violation Engrams)
     8→>
     9→> **Prerequisites**: [02-audit-chain.md](02-audit-chain.md), [11-temporal-logic.md](11-temporal-logic.md)
    10→
    11→
    12→> **Implementation**: Specified
    13→
    14→---
    15→
    16→## Overview
    17→
    18→The existing audit chain in Roko is a linear Merkle hash-chain: each decision is hashed, each hash commits to the previous one. This chain proves that events happened in a particular sequence. It cannot prove **why** those events happened. A linear chain records that the agent swapped ETH for USDC at block 19,412,003. It says nothing about the three observations that suggested a regime change, the two predictions that confirmed it, or the Gate that approved the trade.
    19→
    20→The **Witness DAG** extends the linear audit chain into a directed acyclic graph that links every observation, prediction, decision, and outcome into a tamper-proof chain of reasoning. Any learned knowledge in the Neuro store (Roko's knowledge management subsystem, `roko-neuro`) traces backward through the DAG to the raw observations that justify it. The linear audit chain becomes a degenerate path through the DAG — backward compatibility is preserved.
    21→
    22→This document specifies the Witness DAG's mathematical foundations, data structures, zero-knowledge proof capabilities, storage model, and integration with the rest of the Roko architecture.
    23→
    24→---
    25→
    26→## The Problem: Four Gaps in the Linear Chain
    27→
    28→### Gap 1: No Reasoning Provenance
    29→
    30→The audit chain records that the agent performed an action. It does not record which observations, predictions, and risk assessments led to that action. Post-mortem analysis can determine *what* happened but not *why* the agent thought it was appropriate.
    31→
    32→In a coding domain: the chain records that the agent modified `auth.rs` and added a new endpoint. It does not record which code intelligence signals, which test failures, which PRD requirements, or which Neuro entries informed that implementation choice.
    33→
    34→### Gap 2: No Knowledge Provenance
    35→
    36→The Neuro store (Roko's persistent knowledge system, `roko-neuro`) contains entries such as "high code churn in authentication modules correlates with security vulnerabilities" or "momentum strategies fail in range-bound markets." Which episodes taught these lessons? How many observations support them? There is no way to trace a Neuro entry back to its evidential basis without the DAG.
    37→
    38→### Gap 3: Trust Requires Reputation
    39→
    40→When agents in a Collective (a group of cooperating agents on the Korai network) establish trust, they rely on reputation scores and attestations. Reputation is backward-looking and gameable: an agent can build reputation through conservative behavior, then exploit that trust. **Verifiable reasoning quality** — cryptographic proof that decisions were grounded in real observations — would be a stronger trust signal than reputation alone.
    41→
    42→### Gap 4: Auditing Requires Revelation
    43→
    44→Stakeholders (depositors, operators, regulators) want to audit decision quality. Today this requires revealing the strategy itself. There is no way to prove "my decisions were well-reasoned" without showing the reasoning — unless zero-knowledge proofs are used (see §Zero-Knowledge Proofs below).
    45→
    46→---
    47→
    48→## Mathematical Foundations
    49→
    50→### DAG Structure
    51→
    52→A Witness DAG is a directed acyclic graph **W = (V, E)** where:
    53→
    54→**Vertices V:** Every cognitive event produces a vertex. Five vertex types map to steps of the Universal Cognitive Loop (the 9-step Synapse Loop that every Roko agent runs at its own timescale):
    55→
    56→| Type | Label | Created at Loop Step | Description |
    57→|---|---|---|---|
    58→| Observation | O | Step 1 (PERCEIVE) | Raw perceptual data: price feeds, on-chain events, code intelligence signals, test results, file changes |
    59→| Prediction | P | Step 3 (ATTEND) | Forecasts derived from observations: "this refactoring will break 3 tests" or "ETH will decline 3% in 10 ticks" |
    60→| Decision | D | Steps 4-6 (INTEGRATE, ACT, VERIFY) | Actions chosen based on predictions: "modify auth.rs" or "swap 10 ETH for USDC" |
    61→| Resolution | R | Step 8 (ADAPT) | Observed outcomes: "compilation succeeded, 2 tests failed" or "swap executed at 3,201" |
    62→| NeuroEntry | G | Step 9 (META-COGNIZE) | Learned knowledge: "this pattern of test failure indicates missing error handling" |
    63→
    64→Note: The legacy specification used "GrimoireEntry" for the fifth vertex type. In the current architecture, Grimoire has been renamed to Neuro (`roko-neuro`), so this type is "NeuroEntry" — a learned knowledge entry persisted in the Neuro store.
    65→
    66→**Edges E:** Directed edges encode "was used to produce." Direction points from input to output:
    67→
    68→- **O → P**: "Observation O was used to generate prediction P."
    69→- **P → D**: "Prediction P informed decision D."
    70→- **D → R**: "Decision D produced resolution R."
    71→- **P → G**: "Prediction P contributed to Neuro entry G."
    72→- **R → G**: "Resolution R contributed to Neuro entry G."
    73→- **G → P**: "Neuro entry G influenced prediction P." (Knowledge feedback loop.)
    74→
    75→### Cryptographic Commitment
    76→
    77→Each vertex carries two hashes:
    78→
    79→**Content hash** — commits to the vertex's data. Two vertices with identical content produce identical content hashes:
    80→
    81→```
    82→h(v) = BLAKE3(type || timestamp || content(v))
    83→```
    84→
    85→**Commitment hash** — commits to both the vertex's content and its entire ancestry. This is the Merkle property: modifying any vertex invalidates the commitment hashes of all its descendants:
    86→
    87→```
    88→c(v) = BLAKE3(h(v) || c(parent_1) || c(parent_2) || ... || c(parent_n))
    89→```
    90→
    91→Parent commitment hashes are sorted lexicographically before hashing to ensure deterministic commitment regardless of edge insertion order.
    92→
    93→**Why BLAKE3:** BLAKE3 is chosen over SHA-256 for witness hashing: 3-5x faster on modern hardware, tree-based structure enables incremental hashing of event streams, and 256-bit output provides equivalent collision resistance. This choice is consistent with the Engram's `ContentHash` which also uses BLAKE3 (see the Engram struct in `roko-core`).
    94→
    95→### Tamper Evidence
    96→
    97→The commitment hash `c(v)` of any vertex commits to the entire subgraph that produced it. If an attacker modifies observation O_17 that was used to generate prediction P_8, then:
    98→
    99→1. `c(O_17)` changes because the content changed
   100→2. `c(P_8)` changes because it includes `c(O_17)` as a parent
   101→3. Every decision, resolution, and Neuro entry downstream of P_8 has an invalid commitment hash
   102→
   103→The root hash (most recent vertex, or a synthetic root committing to all leaves) summarizes the entire reasoning history. Publishing this root to an external system (a blockchain such as Korai, a timestamping service) creates a non-repudiable commitment to the complete reasoning chain.
   104→
   105→The existing linear audit chain is a special case: if every vertex has exactly one parent and the only vertex type is D, the Witness DAG reduces to a linear hash chain. The DAG is a strict generalization.
   106→
   107→### Hallucination vs. Memory Detection
   108→
   109→The DAG enables distinguishing two failure modes that look identical in a linear chain:
   110→
   111→**Hallucination.** A decision D has no observation vertices in its provenance subgraph. The agent made a decision based on fabricated or injected data rather than real observations. The DAG detects this: `observation_provenance(D)` returns an empty set.
   112→
   113→**Memory corruption.** A Neuro entry G has valid observation provenance, but the commitment hashes in the chain are invalid. Something tampered with the reasoning chain after the fact. The DAG detects this: `verify(G)` returns false.
   114→
   115→**Stale knowledge.** A Neuro entry G has valid provenance, but the observations in its chain are all older than a threshold T. The knowledge is grounded but may be outdated. The DAG quantifies this: `max_observation_age(G)` returns the age of the oldest supporting observation. This integrates with the Neuro tier system — entries whose provenance is entirely stale may be demoted from Consolidated tier to Working tier.
   116→
   117→---
   118→
   119→## Core Data Structures
   120→
   121→### Vertex Type and Vertex
   122→
   123→```rust
   124→use blake3::Hash;
   125→use std::sync::Arc;
   126→
   127→/// The five types of cognitive event that produce DAG vertices.
   128→/// Maps directly to steps in the Universal Cognitive Loop.
   129→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
   130→#[repr(u8)]
   131→pub enum VertexType {
   132→    /// Raw perceptual data from step 1 (PERCEIVE).
   133→    Observation = 0,
   134→    /// Forecasts from step 3 (ATTEND).
   135→    Prediction = 1,
   136→    /// Actions from steps 4-6 (INTEGRATE/ACT/VERIFY).
   137→    Decision = 2,
   138→    /// Observed outcomes from step 8 (ADAPT).
   139→    Resolution = 3,
   140→    /// Learned knowledge from step 9 (META-COGNIZE).
   141→    /// Formerly "GrimoireEntry" — now NeuroEntry per naming map.
   142→    NeuroEntry = 4,
   143→}
   144→
   145→/// A single vertex in the Witness DAG.
   146→#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
   147→pub struct Vertex {
   148→    /// BLAKE3(content_hash || sorted parent commitment hashes).
   149→    /// Commits to the vertex AND its entire ancestry.
   150→    pub commitment_hash: Hash,
   151→    /// BLAKE3(type || timestamp || content).
   152→    /// Commits to the vertex's data only.
   153→    pub content_hash: Hash,
   154→    /// What kind of cognitive event this vertex represents.
   155→    pub vertex_type: VertexType,
   156→    /// When this vertex was created, in milliseconds since epoch.
   157→    pub timestamp_ms: u64,
   158→    /// Serialized content of the cognitive event.
   159→    pub content: Vec<u8>,
   160→    /// Commitment hashes of parent vertices, sorted lexicographically.
   161→    pub parent_hashes: Vec<Hash>,
   162→}
   163→
   164→impl Vertex {
   165→    /// Create a new vertex and compute both hashes.
   166→    pub fn new(
   167→        vertex_type: VertexType,
   168→        timestamp_ms: u64,
   169→        content: Vec<u8>,
   170→        parent_hashes: Vec<Hash>,
   171→    ) -> Self {
   172→        // Content hash: H(type || timestamp || content)
   173→        let content_hash = {
   174→            let mut hasher = blake3::Hasher::new();
   175→            hasher.update(&[vertex_type as u8]);
   176→            hasher.update(&timestamp_ms.to_le_bytes());
   177→            hasher.update(&content);
   178→            hasher.finalize()
   179→        };
   180→
   181→        // Sort parent hashes for deterministic commitment
   182→        let mut sorted_parents = parent_hashes;
   183→        sorted_parents.sort_by(|a, b| a.as_bytes().cmp(b.as_bytes()));
   184→
   185→        // Commitment hash: H(content_hash || parent_1 || parent_2 || ...)
   186→        let commitment_hash = {
   187→            let mut hasher = blake3::Hasher::new();
   188→            hasher.update(content_hash.as_bytes());
   189→            for parent in &sorted_parents {
   190→                hasher.update(parent.as_bytes());
   191→            }
   192→            hasher.finalize()
   193→        };
   194→
   195→        Self {
   196→            commitment_hash,
   197→            content_hash,
   198→            vertex_type,
   199→            timestamp_ms,
   200→            content,
   201→            parent_hashes: sorted_parents,
   202→        }
   203→    }
   204→}
   205→```
   206→
   207→### The WitnessDAG Struct
   208→
   209→```rust
   210→use dashmap::DashMap;
   211→use std::sync::atomic::{AtomicU32, Ordering};
   212→
   213→/// The Witness DAG: a content-addressed, append-only DAG of cognitive events.
   214→/// Thread-safe via DashMap (sharded concurrent HashMap) and parking_lot::RwLock.
   215→pub struct WitnessDAG {
   216→    /// All vertices, indexed by commitment hash.
   217→    vertices: DashMap<Hash, Arc<Vertex>>,
   218→    /// Forward edges: parent -> set of children.
   219→    children: DashMap<Hash, Vec<Hash>>,
   220→    /// The commitment hash of the most recently added vertex.
   221→    latest: parking_lot::RwLock<Option<Hash>>,
   222→    /// Maximum depth of any path in the DAG.
   223→    /// Exposed as a signal for Collective peers to read.
   224→    pub dag_depth: AtomicU32,
   225→}
   226→
   227→impl WitnessDAG {
   228→    pub fn new() -> Self {
   229→        Self {
   230→            vertices: DashMap::new(),
   231→            children: DashMap::new(),
   232→            latest: parking_lot::RwLock::new(None),
   233→            dag_depth: AtomicU32::new(0),
   234→        }
   235→    }
   236→
   237→    /// Append a vertex to the DAG. O(1) amortized.
   238→    pub fn append(&self, vertex: Vertex) -> Hash {
   239→        let hash = vertex.commitment_hash;
   240→
   241→        // Register forward edges from each parent to this vertex.
   242→        for parent in &vertex.parent_hashes {
   243→            self.children
   244→                .entry(*parent)
   245→                .or_insert_with(Vec::new)
   246→                .push(hash);
   247→        }
   248→
   249→        // Update depth: max(parent depths) + 1.
   250→        let depth = vertex
   251→            .parent_hashes
   252→            .iter()
   253→            .filter_map(|p| self.vertices.get(p))
   254→            .map(|v| self.vertex_depth(&v.commitment_hash))
   255→            .max()
   256→            .unwrap_or(0)
   257→            + 1;
   258→
   259→        let current_max = self.dag_depth.load(Ordering::Relaxed);
   260→        if depth > current_max {
   261→            self.dag_depth.store(depth, Ordering::Relaxed);
   262→        }
   263→
   264→        self.vertices.insert(hash, Arc::new(vertex));
   265→        *self.latest.write() = Some(hash);
   266→
   267→        hash
   268→    }
   269→
   270→    /// Walk the DAG backward from a vertex, collecting all ancestors.
   271→    /// Used for provenance queries. BFS traversal.
   272→    pub fn provenance(&self, start: &Hash) -> Vec<Arc<Vertex>> {
   273→        let mut visited = std::collections::HashSet::new();
   274→        let mut queue = std::collections::VecDeque::new();
   275→        let mut result = Vec::new();
   276→
   277→        queue.push_back(*start);
   278→
   279→        while let Some(current) = queue.pop_front() {
   280→            if !visited.insert(current) {
   281→                continue;
   282→            }
   283→            if let Some(vertex) = self.vertices.get(&current) {
   284→                for parent in &vertex.parent_hashes {
   285→                    queue.push_back(*parent);
   286→                }
   287→                result.push(Arc::clone(&vertex));
   288→            }
   289→        }
   290→
   291→        result
   292→    }
   293→
   294→    /// Verify the integrity of a vertex: recompute its commitment hash
   295→    /// and check that it matches the stored value.
   296→    pub fn verify(&self, hash: &Hash) -> bool {
   297→        let vertex = match self.vertices.get(hash) {
   298→            Some(v) => v.clone(),
   299→            None => return false,
   300→        };
   301→
   302→        // Recompute content hash
   303→        let expected_content = {
   304→            let mut hasher = blake3::Hasher::new();
   305→            hasher.update(&[vertex.vertex_type as u8]);
   306→            hasher.update(&vertex.timestamp_ms.to_le_bytes());
   307→            hasher.update(&vertex.content);
   308→            hasher.finalize()
   309→        };
   310→
   311→        if expected_content != vertex.content_hash {
   312→            return false;
   313→        }
   314→
   315→        // Recompute commitment hash
   316→        let expected_commitment = {
   317→            let mut hasher = blake3::Hasher::new();
   318→            hasher.update(vertex.content_hash.as_bytes());
   319→            for parent in &vertex.parent_hashes {
   320→                hasher.update(parent.as_bytes());
   321→            }
   322→            hasher.finalize()
   323→        };
   324→
   325→        expected_commitment == vertex.commitment_hash
   326→    }
   327→
   328→    /// Find all observation vertices that support a given vertex.
   329→    pub fn observation_provenance(&self, root: &Hash) -> Vec<Arc<Vertex>> {
   330→        self.provenance(root)
   331→            .into_iter()
   332→            .filter(|v| v.vertex_type == VertexType::Observation)
   333→            .collect()
   334→    }
   335→
   336→    /// Find all prediction-resolution pairs in the provenance of a vertex.
   337→    /// Used for computing prediction accuracy over a reasoning chain.
   338→    pub fn prediction_resolution_pairs(
   339→        &self,
   340→        root: &Hash,
   341→    ) -> Vec<(Arc<Vertex>, Arc<Vertex>)> {
   342→        let ancestors = self.provenance(root);
   343→        let ancestor_set: std::collections::HashSet<Hash> =
   344→            ancestors.iter().map(|v| v.commitment_hash).collect();
   345→
   346→        let mut pairs = Vec::new();
   347→
   348→        for vertex in &ancestors {
   349→            if vertex.vertex_type != VertexType::Prediction {
   350→                continue;
   351→            }
   352→
   353→            if let Some(child_hashes) = self.children.get(&vertex.commitment_hash) {
   354→                for child_hash in child_hashes.iter() {
   355→                    if ancestor_set.contains(child_hash) {
   356→                        if let Some(child) = self.vertices.get(child_hash) {
   357→                            if child.vertex_type == VertexType::Resolution {
   358→                                pairs.push((Arc::clone(vertex), Arc::clone(&child)));
   359→                            }
   360→                        }
   361→                    }
   362→                }
   363→            }
   364→        }
   365→
   366→        pairs
   367→    }
   368→
   369→    fn vertex_depth(&self, hash: &Hash) -> u32 {
   370→        let vertex = match self.vertices.get(hash) {
   371→            Some(v) => v.clone(),
   372→            None => return 0,
   373→        };
   374→        if vertex.parent_hashes.is_empty() {
   375→            return 1;
   376→        }
   377→        vertex
   378→            .parent_hashes
   379→            .iter()
   380→            .map(|p| self.vertex_depth(p))
   381→            .max()
   382→            .unwrap_or(0)
   383→            + 1
   384→    }
   385→}
   386→```
   387→

   389→
   390→## Integration with the Universal Cognitive Loop
   391→
   392→The Witness DAG is constructed incrementally as the 9-step Synapse Loop executes:
   393→
   394→| Loop Step | Step Name | DAG Action |
   395→|---|---|---|
   396→| 1 | PERCEIVE | Create O vertices for each observation. No parents (these are roots). |
   397→| 2 | EVALUATE | No new vertices. Scoring metadata attached to existing vertices. |
   398→| 3 | ATTEND | Create P vertices. Edges from O vertices used and any G (Neuro) entries consulted. |
   399→| 4 | INTEGRATE | Create D vertex if the Composer decides on an action. Edges from P vertices. |
   400→| 5 | ACT | Update D vertex with execution results. No new vertices. |
   401→| 6 | VERIFY | Finalize D vertex. Commitment hash computed at this point via Gate verification. |
   402→| 7 | PERSIST | Store vertex in Substrate. Execution record linked to D. |
   403→| 8 | ADAPT | Create R vertices for each resolution. Edges from D. |
   404→| 9 | META-COGNIZE | Create G (NeuroEntry) vertices for new knowledge. Edges from relevant P, R, D vertices. |
   405→
   406→Each vertex is an Engram — it carries the full Engram metadata (kind, body, tags, score, lineage, provenance). The DAG's commitment hash chain is orthogonal to and consistent with the Engram's own `ContentHash` (which is `BLAKE3(kind + body + author + tags)` per the Engram spec).
   407→
   408→---
   409→
   410→## Zero-Knowledge Proofs for Strategy Auditing
   411→
   412→Using ZK-SNARKs or ZK-STARKs, an agent can prove statements about its DAG structure without revealing DAG contents. Four proof types address the four gaps identified above:
   413→
   414→### Proof 1: Decision Grounding
   415→
   416→"This decision was based on at least N observations and M predictions."
   417→
   418→The prover demonstrates that the subgraph rooted at decision D_i contains at least N observation vertices and M prediction vertices, all with valid commitment hashes. The verifier learns the branching factor but not the content of any vertex. This addresses **Gap 1** (no reasoning provenance) — stakeholders verify decision quality without seeing the strategy.
   419→
   420→### Proof 2: Knowledge Provenance
   421→
   422→"This Neuro entry traces back to at least K direct observations."
   423→
   424→The prover walks the DAG backward from NeuroEntry G_j and proves that the reachable subgraph contains at least K observation vertices. The verifier learns evidential depth but not the observations themselves. This addresses **Gap 2** (no knowledge provenance) — the strength of a knowledge entry is verifiable without revealing what the agent learned.
   425→
   426→### Proof 3: Prediction Accuracy
   427→
   428→"My prediction accuracy over the last T ticks exceeds X%."
   429→
   430→The prover identifies all prediction-resolution pairs in a time window, computes accuracy, and proves the result exceeds the threshold. The verifier learns the accuracy percentage but not individual predictions or resolutions. This addresses **Gap 3** (trust requires reputation) — trust becomes proportional to verifiable prediction quality.
   431→
   432→### Proof 4: Reasoning Consistency
   433→
   434→"All commitment hashes in the subgraph rooted at vertex V are valid."
   435→
   436→Proves the reasoning chain has not been tampered with, without revealing the chain itself. This addresses **Gap 4** (auditing requires revelation).
   437→
   438→**Implementation note:** ZK proof generation is O(circuit_size). A grounding proof for a typical decision with 10-20 parent vertices takes 1-5 seconds using plonky2. Too slow for real-time, acceptable for on-demand auditing. Full ZK integration is deferred to Tier 4 in the implementation roadmap (see `refactoring-prd/07-implementation-priorities.md`).
   439→
