   100→
   101→## BuchiAutomaton: full implementation
   102→
   103→A Buchi automaton accepts or rejects infinite streams of events. Each LTL formula compiles to one automaton.
   104→
   105→```rust
   106→use std::collections::{HashMap, HashSet};
   107→
   108→/// A state in the Buchi automaton.
   109→pub type StateId = u32;
   110→
   111→/// An atomic proposition (e.g., "bash_call", "safety_check", "gate_passed").
   112→pub type Proposition = String;
   113→
   114→/// A set of propositions that are true at a given time step.
   115→pub type EventLabel = HashSet<Proposition>;
   116→
   117→/// A compiled Buchi automaton for monitoring a single LTL formula.
   118→pub struct BuchiAutomaton {
   119→    /// Human-readable name of the formula being monitored.
   120→    formula_name: String,
   121→    /// The original LTL formula string (for diagnostics).
   122→    formula_source: String,
   123→    /// Number of states in the automaton.
   124→    num_states: u32,
   125→    /// Initial state.
   126→    initial_state: StateId,
   127→    /// Accepting states (the automaton must visit these infinitely often).
   128→    accepting_states: HashSet<StateId>,
   129→    /// Transition function: (current_state, event_label) -> set of next states.
   130→    /// Nondeterministic: a single event can lead to multiple states.
   131→    transitions: HashMap<(StateId, EventLabel), HashSet<StateId>>,
   132→    /// Propositions this automaton cares about.
   133→    alphabet: HashSet<Proposition>,
   134→}
   135→
   136→impl BuchiAutomaton {
   137→    pub fn formula_name(&self) -> &str {
   138→        &self.formula_name
   139→    }
   140→
   141→    /// Compute the set of next states given a current state and event.
   142→    /// For nondeterministic automata, returns all reachable states.
   143→    pub fn transition(
   144→        &self,
   145→        current: &AutomatonState,
   146→        event: &EventLabel,
   147→    ) -> AutomatonState {
   148→        let mut next_states = HashSet::new();
   149→
   150→        // Filter event to only propositions this automaton uses.
   151→        let relevant: EventLabel = event
   152→            .iter()
   153→            .filter(|p| self.alphabet.contains(*p))
   154→            .cloned()
   155→            .collect();
   156→
   157→        for &state in &current.active_states {
   158→            if let Some(targets) = self.transitions.get(&(state, relevant.clone())) {
   159→                next_states.extend(targets);
   160→            }
   161→        }
   162→
   163→        // If no transitions matched, the automaton is stuck (rejecting).
   164→        let in_accepting = next_states
   165→            .iter()
   166→            .any(|s| self.accepting_states.contains(s));
   167→
   168→        AutomatonState {
   169→            active_states: next_states,
   170→            visited_accepting: current.visited_accepting || in_accepting,
   171→            steps_since_accepting: if in_accepting {
   172→                0
   173→            } else {
   174→                current.steps_since_accepting + 1
   175→            },
   176→        }
   177→    }
   178→
   179→    /// Check whether the automaton is in a rejecting configuration.
   180→    ///
   181→    /// A state is rejecting when:
   182→    /// - The active state set is empty (no valid transitions exist), OR
   183→    /// - The automaton has not visited an accepting state in
   184→    ///   `liveness_bound` steps (bounded liveness check).
   185→    pub fn is_rejecting(&self, state: &AutomatonState) -> bool {
   186→        // Dead: no active states remain.
   187→        if state.active_states.is_empty() {
   188→            return true;
   189→        }
   190→
   191→        // Bounded liveness: if we haven't seen an accepting state
   192→        // in too long, treat as a violation. This converts liveness
   193→        // properties to safety properties with a finite bound.
   194→        // Default bound: 1000 steps (~2.5 hours at gamma speed).
   195→        let liveness_bound = 1000;
   196→        state.steps_since_accepting > liveness_bound
   197→    }
   198→}
   199→
   200→/// Runtime state of a monitored automaton.
   201→#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
   202→pub struct AutomatonState {
   203→    /// Current active states (nondeterministic: may be multiple).
   204→    pub active_states: HashSet<StateId>,
   205→    /// Whether an accepting state has been visited at least once.
   206→    pub visited_accepting: bool,
   207→    /// Number of steps since the last visit to an accepting state.
   208→    pub steps_since_accepting: u64,
   209→}
   210→
   211→impl AutomatonState {
   212→    /// Create an initial state from the automaton's initial state.
   213→    pub fn initial(automaton: &BuchiAutomaton) -> Self {
   214→        let mut active = HashSet::new();
   215→        active.insert(automaton.initial_state);
   216→        Self {
   217→            active_states: active,
   218→            visited_accepting: automaton
   219→                .accepting_states
   220→                .contains(&automaton.initial_state),
   221→            steps_since_accepting: 0,
   222→        }
   223→    }
   224→}
   225→```
   226→
   227→### TemporalMonitor: full struct
   228→
   229→```rust
   230→/// Runtime temporal monitor. Runs all compiled LTL automata
   231→/// against the stream of Engrams at each gamma tick.
   232→pub struct TemporalMonitor {
   233→    /// Compiled LTL formulas as Buchi automata.
   234→    automata: Vec<BuchiAutomaton>,
   235→    /// Current runtime state for each automaton.
   236→    states: Vec<AutomatonState>,
   237→    /// Event classifier: maps Engram kinds to propositions.
   238→    classifier: EventClassifier,
   239→    /// Liveness bound: max steps without visiting an accepting state.
   240→    /// Default: 1000 (~2.5 hours at gamma speed).
   241→    liveness_bound: u64,
   242→    /// Total events processed (for diagnostics).
   243→    events_processed: u64,
   244→    /// Total violations detected.
   245→    violations_detected: u64,
   246→}
   247→
   248→impl TemporalMonitor {
   249→    pub fn new(liveness_bound: u64) -> Self {
   250→        Self {
   251→            automata: Vec::new(),
   252→            states: Vec::new(),
   253→            classifier: EventClassifier::default(),
   254→            liveness_bound,
   255→            events_processed: 0,
   256→            violations_detected: 0,
   257→        }
   258→    }
   259→
   260→    /// Add a compiled LTL formula to the monitor.
   261→    pub fn add_formula(&mut self, automaton: BuchiAutomaton) {
   262→        let state = AutomatonState::initial(&automaton);
   263→        self.states.push(state);
   264→        self.automata.push(automaton);
   265→    }
   266→
   267→    /// Process a batch of Engrams (one gamma tick's worth).
   268→    /// Returns violation Engrams for any formulas that entered
   269→    /// a rejecting state.
   270→    pub fn process(&mut self, engrams: &[Signal]) -> Vec<Signal> {
   271→        let mut violations = Vec::new();
   272→
   273→        for engram in engrams {
   274→            self.events_processed += 1;
   275→            let event = self.classifier.classify(engram);
   276→
   277→            for (i, automaton) in self.automata.iter().enumerate() {
   278→                let new_state = automaton.transition(&self.states[i], &event);
   279→                if automaton.is_rejecting(&new_state) {
   280→                    self.violations_detected += 1;
   281→                    violations.push(create_violation_engram(
   282→                        automaton.formula_name(),
   283→                        engram,
   284→                    ));
   285→                }
   286→                self.states[i] = new_state;
   287→            }
   288→        }
   289→
   290→        violations
   291→    }
   292→
   293→    /// Reset all automata to their initial states.
   294→    /// Used after a Pause/Resume cycle.
   295→    pub fn reset(&mut self) {
   296→        for (i, automaton) in self.automata.iter().enumerate() {
   297→            self.states[i] = AutomatonState::initial(automaton);
   298→        }
   299→    }
   300→}
   301→
   302→/// Maps Engram kinds and metadata to atomic propositions.
   303→struct EventClassifier {
   304→    /// Mapping from (Engram kind, optional tool name) to proposition set.
   305→    rules: Vec<ClassificationRule>,
   306→}
   307→
   308→struct ClassificationRule {
   309→    kind_match: Option<String>,    // Engram kind to match.
   310→    tool_match: Option<String>,    // Tool name to match (for tool call Engrams).
   311→    propositions: Vec<Proposition>, // Propositions to emit on match.
   312→}
   313→```
   314→
   315→### LTL formula parser and compiler
   316→
   317→The parser converts LTL formula strings into `BuchiAutomaton` instances using a two-stage pipeline:
   318→
   319→```
   320→"G(bash_call -> previous(safety_check))"
   321→    |
   322→    v
   323→  [Parser] --> LtlAst
   324→    |
   325→    v
   326→  [Compiler] --> BuchiAutomaton (via Gerth et al. 1995 algorithm)
   327→```
   328→
   329→**LTL AST:**
   330→
   331→```rust
   332→/// Abstract syntax tree for LTL formulas.
   333→pub enum LtlAst {
   334→    /// Atomic proposition: "bash_call", "gate_passed", etc.
   335→    Atom(Proposition),
   336→    /// Negation: not phi.
   337→    Not(Box<LtlAst>),
   338→    /// Conjunction: phi and psi.
   339→    And(Box<LtlAst>, Box<LtlAst>),
   340→    /// Disjunction: phi or psi.
   341→    Or(Box<LtlAst>, Box<LtlAst>),
   342→    /// Implication: phi -> psi (sugar for !phi || psi).
   343→    Implies(Box<LtlAst>, Box<LtlAst>),
   344→    /// Globally: G(phi) -- phi holds at every future step.
   345→    Globally(Box<LtlAst>),
   346→    /// Eventually: F(phi) -- phi holds at some future step.
   347→    Eventually(Box<LtlAst>),
   348→    /// Next: X(phi) -- phi holds at the next step.
   349→    Next(Box<LtlAst>),
   350→    /// Until: phi U psi -- phi holds until psi becomes true.
   351→    Until(Box<LtlAst>, Box<LtlAst>),
   352→    /// Previous: P(phi) -- phi held at the previous step (past-time LTL).
   353→    Previous(Box<LtlAst>),
   354→}
   355→```
   356→
   357→**Compiler algorithm** (Gerth, Peled, Vardi, Wolper 1995):
   358→
   359→```
   360→ltl_to_buchi(formula: LtlAst) -> BuchiAutomaton:
   361→    # Step 1: Negate the formula (Buchi automaton accepts violations).
   362→    negated = negate(formula)
   363→
   364→    # Step 2: Convert to negation normal form (push negation inward).
   365→    nnf = to_nnf(negated)
   366→
   367→    # Step 3: Build generalized Buchi automaton (GBA) using the
   368→    #         tableau construction. Each node in the tableau becomes
   369→    #         a state. Transitions are generated by expanding temporal
   370→    #         operators.
   371→    gba = expand_tableau(nnf)
   372→
   373→    # Step 4: Degenralize GBA to standard Buchi automaton.
   374→    #         Uses the standard product construction with acceptance
   375→    #         counter: k acceptance sets become k copies of the state
   376→    #         space, cycling through acceptance sets.
   377→    ba = degeneralize(gba)
   378→
   379→    # Step 5: Minimize (optional, improves runtime monitoring speed).
   380→    #         Remove unreachable states and merge bisimilar states.
   381→    minimized = minimize(ba)
   382→
   383→    return minimized
   384→```
   385→
   386→### CTL pre-execution verification
   387→
   388→CTL model checking verifies properties of execution plans (DAGs of tasks) before they run. The plan's task graph is the model; CTL formulas specify required properties.
   389→
   390→**Algorithm** (Clarke, Emerson, Sistla 1986):
   391→
   392→```
   393→ctl_check(plan: TaskDAG, formula: CtlFormula) -> bool:
   394→    # The plan is a Kripke structure where:
   395→    #   - States = task nodes + a "done" terminal state
   396→    #   - Transitions = task dependencies (edges in the DAG)
   397→    #   - Atomic propositions = task properties (has_gate_check, is_safe, etc.)
   398→
   399→    match formula:
   400→        AG(phi):
   401→            # For all paths, globally phi.
   402→            # Compute the set of states satisfying phi, then check
   403→            # that all reachable states from the initial state are in that set.
   404→            sat_phi = ctl_sat(plan, phi)
   405→            reachable = bfs_reachable(plan, plan.initial_state)
   406→            return reachable.is_subset(&sat_phi)
   407→
   408→        EF(phi):
   409→            # There exists a path where eventually phi.
   410→            # Backward fixed-point: start from states satisfying phi,
   411→            # iteratively add predecessors until fixed point.
   412→            sat_phi = ctl_sat(plan, phi)
   413→            reachable_back = backward_reachable(plan, sat_phi)
   414→            return plan.initial_state in reachable_back
   415→
   416→        AF(phi):
   417→            # For all paths, eventually phi.
   418→            # Backward fixed-point with universal quantification.
   419→            sat_phi = ctl_sat(plan, phi)
   420→            result = sat_phi.clone()
   421→            loop:
   422→                prev = result.clone()
   423→                for state in plan.states:
   424→                    if all successors of state are in result:
   425→                        result.insert(state)
   426→                if result == prev:
   427→                    break
   428→            return plan.initial_state in result
   429→
   430→        AG(EF(safe_state)):
   431→            # From every reachable state, there exists a path to safety.
   432→            # Combined: check AG and EF composition.
   433→            sat_safe = ctl_sat(plan, Atom("safe_state"))
   434→            recoverable = backward_reachable(plan, sat_safe)
   435→            reachable = bfs_reachable(plan, plan.initial_state)
   436→            return reachable.is_subset(&recoverable)
   437→```
   438→
   439→**Standard plan verification properties:**
   440→
   441→| Property | CTL formula | What it checks |
   442→|----------|-------------|---------------|
   443→| No dead ends | `AG(EF(done))` | Every plan state can reach completion |
   444→| Gate coverage | `AG(task_node -> AF(gate_check))` | Every task is eventually followed by a gate check |
   445→| Termination | `not EF(AG(not done))` | No infinite non-terminating path exists |
   446→| Recoverability | `AG(EF(safe_state))` | Every state can reach a safe configuration |
   447→
   448→### Integration with Gate pipeline
   449→
   450→The `TemporalMonitor` integrates as a Policy within the existing Gate/conductor architecture:
   451→
   452→```
   453→Gate pipeline (per-task):
   454→  CompileGate -> TestGate -> ClippyGate -> DiffGate
   455→                                              |
   456→                                              v
   457→Conductor (continuous):                   TemporalMonitor
   458→  circuit breaker <-- health score <-- violation Engrams
   459→       |
   460→       v
   461→  Cooldown / Pause / Shutdown signals
   462→```
   463→
   464→```rust
   465→/// TemporalMonitor as a Gate implementation.
   466→/// Wraps the monitor and checks accumulated temporal properties
   467→/// after each task completes.
   468→pub struct TemporalGate {
   469→    monitor: Arc<parking_lot::Mutex<TemporalMonitor>>,
   470→}
   471→
   472→#[async_trait]
   473→impl Gate for TemporalGate {
   474→    async fn verify(&self, engram: &Signal) -> Result<Verdict> {
   475→        let mut monitor = self.monitor.lock();
   476→        let violations = monitor.process(&[engram.clone()]);
   477→
   478→        if violations.is_empty() {
   479→            Ok(Verdict::Pass {
   480→                confidence: 1.0,
   481→                message: "All temporal properties hold".into(),
   482→            })
   483→        } else {
   484→            Ok(Verdict::Fail {
   485→                confidence: 1.0,
   486→                message: format!(
   487→                    "{} temporal violations: {}",
   488→                    violations.len(),
   489→                    violations
   490→                        .iter()
   491→                        .map(|v| v.body_text().unwrap_or_default())
   492→                        .collect::<Vec<_>>()
   493→                        .join(", ")
   494→                ),
   495→                violations,
   496→            })
   497→        }
   498→    }
   499→}
   500→```
   501→
   502→### Configuration parameters
   503→
   504→```toml
   505→[safety.temporal]
   506→liveness_bound = 1000            # Max steps without accepting state. Range: 100..100000.
   507→tick_interval_secs = 10          # Gamma tick interval. Range: 1..60.
   508→max_formulas = 50                # Maximum compiled automata. Range: 1..200.
   509→violation_priority = "high"      # Priority of violation Engrams: "low", "medium", "high".
   510→```
   511→
   512→### Test criteria
   513→
   514→- `BuchiAutomaton::transition()` with an empty event label produces a valid state transition
   515→- `BuchiAutomaton::is_rejecting()` returns true when active_states is empty
   516→- `BuchiAutomaton::is_rejecting()` returns true when steps_since_accepting exceeds liveness_bound
   517→- `TemporalMonitor::process()` returns a violation Engram when a safety property is violated
   518→- The LTL formula `G(a -> X(b))` compiled to a Buchi automaton rejects the trace `[{a}, {}, {a}, {b}]` (a without b at next step)
   519→- CTL `AG(EF(done))` returns false for a plan DAG with a dead-end node
   520→- `TemporalGate` produces `Verdict::Fail` when any monitored formula is violated
   521→- `TemporalMonitor::reset()` returns all automata to their initial states
   522→

   527→The legacy specification catalogs 40 DeFi temporal patterns. Representative examples:

   533→    "An approval is always followed by a transfer in the next step"

   535→
   536→G(large_swap → ¬X(same_pool_swap))

   539→```
   540→
   541→### Flash Loan Detection

   544→G(borrow → F≤1block(repay))
   545→    "Every borrow is repaid within the same block"
   546→    (characterizes flash loans — legitimate pattern but requires monitoring)
   547→```
   548→
   549→### Liquidation Patterns
   550→
   551→```
   552→G(health_factor_below_1 → F≤5blocks(liquidation ∨ collateral_top_up))
   553→    "A below-threshold health factor leads to liquidation or top-up within 5 blocks"

   557→
   558→## Category-Theoretic Composition
   559→
   560→Temporal monitors compose using category-theoretic operations:
   561→
   562→- **Product**: Monitor A ∧ Monitor B — both must accept
   563→- **Coproduct**: Monitor A ∨ Monitor B — at least one must accept
   564→- **Sequential composition**: Monitor A ; Monitor B — B starts when A accepts
   565→
   566→This enables building complex temporal safety properties from simple, well-tested components. The gate pipeline in `roko-gate` already uses sequential composition: CompileGate → TestGate → ClippyGate.
   567→
   568→---
   569→
   570→## Integration with Roko Safety Architecture
   571→
   572→### Temporal Monitor as a Policy
   573→
   574→In Roko's Synapse Architecture, the temporal monitor implements the `Policy` trait. It observes the stream of Engrams (tool calls, gate verdicts, state changes) and emits new Engrams when temporal properties are violated:
   575→
   576→```rust
   577→pub struct TemporalMonitor {
   578→    /// Compiled LTL formulas as Buchi automata.
   579→    automata: Vec<BuchiAutomaton>,
   580→    /// Current state for each automaton.
   581→    states: Vec<AutomatonState>,
   582→}
   583→
   584→impl Policy for TemporalMonitor {
   585→    fn decide(&self, engrams: &[Signal]) -> Vec<Signal> {
   586→        let mut violations = Vec::new();
   587→        for engram in engrams {
   588→            for (automaton, state) in self.automata.iter().zip(self.states.iter_mut()) {
   589→                let event = classify_event(engram);
   590→                let new_state = automaton.transition(state, &event);
   591→                if automaton.is_rejecting(&new_state) {
   592→                    violations.push(create_violation_engram(
   593→                        automaton.formula_name(),
   594→                        engram,
   595→                    ));
   596→                }
   597→                *state = new_state;
   598→            }
   599→        }
   600→        violations
   601→    }
   602→}
   603→```
   604→
   605→Violation Engrams carry `Kind::SafetyViolation` and high-priority scores, ensuring they are routed to the conductor for intervention. The temporal monitor runs at every gamma tick (~5-15 seconds), checking accumulated events against all active formulas.
   606→
   607→### Existing Temporal Properties in Production
   608→
   609→Several temporal safety properties are already enforced in the Roko codebase, though not expressed as formal LTL formulas:
   610→
   611→| Property | Expression | Enforced By |
   612→|---|---|---|
   613→| "Every bash command is checked before execution" | `G(bash_call → previous(safety_check))` | SafetyLayer pre-execution hook |
   614→| "Gate failures lead to retry or skip" | `G(gate_failed → F(retry ∨ skip ∨ abort))` | Conductor circuit breaker |
   615→| "Every started task eventually terminates" | `G(task_started → F(task_completed ∨ task_failed))` | ProcessSupervisor timeout + ghost turn detection |
   616→| "Rate limits are enforced" | `G(rate_exceeded → ¬tool_call)` | RateLimiter deny response |
   617→
   618→Formalizing these as LTL and compiling to Buchi automata (Tier 3) would make them composable, testable, and extensible.

   652→
   653→## Extended Temporal Pattern Library
   654→
   655→Beyond the existing DeFi patterns, this section defines a comprehensive pattern library for general-purpose coding agents. These are LTL formulas that can be compiled to Buchi automata and loaded into the TemporalMonitor.
   656→
   657→### Code agent safety patterns
   658→
   659→```
   660→% P1: No write without prior read (understand before modifying)
   661→G(write_file(F) -> P(read_file(F)))
   662→    "Every file write was preceded by reading the same file"
   663→
   664→% P2: No git push without passing gates
   665→G(git_push -> P(compile_gate_passed /\ test_gate_passed /\ clippy_gate_passed))
   666→    "Every push was preceded by passing all three gates"
   667→
   668→% P3: No concurrent writes to same file
   669→G(write_file(F) -> -X(write_file(F) /\ different_agent))
   670→    "Two agents never write the same file in consecutive steps"
   671→
   672→% P4: Monotonic progress -- task state only moves forward
   673→G(task_state(T, completed) -> G(task_state(T, completed)))
   674→    "A completed task stays completed (no regression)"
   675→
   676→% P5: Budget enforcement
   677→G(cost_budget_exceeded -> F<=3(pause \/ shutdown))
   678→    "Budget exceeded leads to pause or shutdown within 3 steps"
   679→
   680→% P6: Escalation chain -- failures trigger review
   681→G(gate_failed(T) /\ gate_failed(T) /\ gate_failed(T) -> F(human_review(T)))
   682→    "Three consecutive gate failures trigger human review"
   683→
   684→% P7: Sandbox containment
   685→G(-(file_access /\ outside_worktree))
   686→    "No file access targets paths outside the worktree -- ever"
   687→
   688→% P8: Secret non-disclosure
   689→G(scrub_policy_match -> -X(content_in_llm_context(matched_content)))
   690→    "Scrubbed content never appears in subsequent LLM context"
   691→```
   692→
   693→### Multi-agent coordination patterns
   694→
   695→```
   696→% P9: No conflicting concurrent modifications
   697→G(agent_a_modifies(F) /\ agent_b_modifies(F) -> same_worktree(a, b) = false)
   698→    "Concurrent modifications to the same file only happen in separate worktrees"
   699→
   700→% P10: Task ordering respects DAG dependencies
   701→G(task_started(T2) -> P(task_completed(dep(T2))))
   702→    "A task starts only after all its dependencies have completed"
   703→
   704→% P11: Merge conflict resolution
   705→G(merge_conflict(B) -> F<=10(conflict_resolved(B) \/ merge_aborted(B)))
   706→    "Every merge conflict is resolved or aborted within 10 steps"
   707→```
   708→
   709→### Pattern library registry
   710→
   711→```rust
   712→/// A temporal pattern in the library.
   713→pub struct TemporalPattern {
   714→    /// Unique identifier for this pattern.
   715→    pub id: String,
   716→    /// Human-readable description.
   717→    pub description: String,
   718→    /// The LTL formula source string.
   719→    pub formula: String,
   720→    /// Category: safety, liveness, fairness, coordination.
   721→    pub category: PatternCategory,
   722→    /// Whether this pattern is enabled by default.
   723→    pub enabled_by_default: bool,
   724→    /// Domain: general, code, chain, research.
   725→    pub domain: PatternDomain,
   726→    /// Severity of a violation.
   727→    pub violation_severity: ViolationSeverity,
   728→}
   729→
   730→pub enum PatternCategory {
   731→    Safety,       // G(-bad) -- something bad never happens
   732→    Liveness,     // G(started -> F(completed)) -- something good eventually happens
   733→    Fairness,     // GF(condition) -- something happens infinitely often
   734→    Coordination, // Multi-agent ordering constraints
   735→}
   736→
   737→pub enum PatternDomain { General, Code, Chain, Research }
   738→pub enum ViolationSeverity { Info, Warning, Error, Critical }
   739→
   740→/// The pattern library: a registry of all temporal patterns.
   741→pub struct PatternLibrary {
   742→    patterns: Vec<TemporalPattern>,
   743→}
   744→
   745→impl PatternLibrary {
   746→    /// Load the default pattern library for a given domain.
   747→    pub fn default_for_domain(domain: PatternDomain) -> Self {
   748→        let mut lib = Self { patterns: Vec::new() };
   749→        // Always include general safety patterns
   750→        lib.add_general_patterns();
   751→        match domain {
   752→            PatternDomain::Code => lib.add_code_patterns(),
   753→            PatternDomain::Chain => lib.add_chain_patterns(),
   754→            _ => {}
   755→        }
   756→        lib
   757→    }
   758→
   759→    /// Compile all enabled patterns into Buchi automata.
   760→    pub fn compile_enabled(&self) -> Vec<BuchiAutomaton> {
   761→        self.patterns
   762→            .iter()
   763→            .filter(|p| p.enabled_by_default)
   764→            .map(|p| ltl_to_buchi(&parse_ltl(&p.formula)))
   765→            .collect()
   766→    }
   767→}
   768→```
   769→
   770→### Past-time LTL (ptLTL) extensions
   771→
   772→Standard LTL looks forward in time (G, F, X, U). Past-time LTL adds backward-looking operators that are essential for safety patterns like "every write was preceded by a read."
   773→
   774→```rust
   775→/// Past-time LTL operators.
   776→/// These extend the LtlAst with backward-looking operators.
   777→pub enum PtLtlOp {
   778→    /// Previously: the formula held at some past step.
   779→    Previously(Box<LtlAst>),
   780→    /// Once: the formula held at some past step (past-time F).
   781→    Once(Box<LtlAst>),
   782→    /// Historically: the formula held at every past step (past-time G).
   783→    Historically(Box<LtlAst>),
   784→    /// Since: phi held since psi was true (past-time U).
   785→    Since(Box<LtlAst>, Box<LtlAst>),
   786→}
   787→```
   788→
   789→Past-time LTL formulas can be monitored in O(|formula|) space per step (Havelund & Rosu, 2004), making them efficient for real-time monitoring.
   790→
   791→### Bounded temporal operators
   792→
   793→Many safety patterns use bounded temporal operators (F<=k, G<=k) for practical monitoring:
   794→
   795→```rust
   796→/// Bounded temporal operators: F<=k and G<=k.
   797→/// These convert liveness properties to bounded-liveness safety properties.
   798→pub enum BoundedOp {
   799→    /// Eventually within k steps.
   800→    EventuallyBounded { formula: Box<LtlAst>, bound: u64 },
   801→    /// Globally for the next k steps.
   802→    GloballyBounded { formula: Box<LtlAst>, bound: u64 },
   803→}
   804→```
   805→
   806→### Configuration
   807→
   808→```toml
   809→[safety.temporal.patterns]
   810→# Domain for pattern library selection.
   811→domain = "code"    # "general" | "code" | "chain" | "research"
   812→# Additional custom pattern files (LTL formulas, one per line).
   813→custom_pattern_files = [".roko/temporal-patterns.ltl"]
   814→# Enable past-time LTL operators.
   815→enable_ptltl = true
   816→# Maximum bound for bounded temporal operators.
   817→max_bounded_horizon = 100    # Range: 10..10000.
   818→# Pattern violation response.
   819→violation_response = "alert"  # "log" | "alert" | "pause" | "abort"
   820→```
   821→
   822→### Test criteria
   823→
   824→- Pattern P1 (write-after-read) rejects trace [{write_file("a.rs")}, ...] without prior read_file("a.rs")
   825→- Pattern P2 (push-after-gates) rejects trace with git_push but no prior gate passes
   826→- Pattern P7 (sandbox containment) rejects any trace containing file_access + outside_worktree
   827→- PatternLibrary::default_for_domain(Code) includes all code patterns
   828→- PatternLibrary::compile_enabled() produces valid Buchi automata for each pattern
   829→- Past-time Once(phi) is satisfied iff phi held at some earlier step
   830→- Bounded F<=5(phi) is violated if phi does not hold within 5 steps
   831→- Pattern violation severity is correctly propagated to the TemporalMonitor's violation Engrams
   832→

1→# Witness DAG: Cryptographic Cognitive Traces
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L3 Harness (audit chain extension), L4 Orchestration (cross-agent verification)
     4→>
     5→> **Crate**: Target: `roko-gate` (extension of existing audit chain), integration with `roko-fs` (SQLite storage)
     6→>
     7→> **Synapse traits**: `Substrate` (persist vertices), `Gate` (verify commitment hashes), `Policy` (emit DAG violation Engrams)
     8→>
     9→> **Prerequisites**: [02-audit-chain.md](02-audit-chain.md), [11-temporal-logic.md](11-temporal-logic.md)
    10→
    11→
    12→> **Implementation**: Specified
    13→
    14→---
    15→
    16→## Overview
    17→
    18→The existing audit chain in Roko is a linear Merkle hash-chain: each decision is hashed, each hash commits to the previous one. This chain proves that events happened in a particular sequence. It cannot prove **why** those events happened. A linear chain records that the agent swapped ETH for USDC at block 19,412,003. It says nothing about the three observations that suggested a regime change, the two predictions that confirmed it, or the Gate that approved the trade.
    19→
    20→The **Witness DAG** extends the linear audit chain into a directed acyclic graph that links every observation, prediction, decision, and outcome into a tamper-proof chain of reasoning. Any learned knowledge in the Neuro store (Roko's knowledge management subsystem, `roko-neuro`) traces backward through the DAG to the raw observations that justify it. The linear audit chain becomes a degenerate path through the DAG — backward compatibility is preserved.
    21→
    22→This document specifies the Witness DAG's mathematical foundations, data structures, zero-knowledge proof capabilities, storage model, and integration with the rest of the Roko architecture.
    23→
    24→---
    25→
    26→## The Problem: Four Gaps in the Linear Chain
    27→
    28→### Gap 1: No Reasoning Provenance
    29→
    30→The audit chain records that the agent performed an action. It does not record which observations, predictions, and risk assessments led to that action. Post-mortem analysis can determine *what* happened but not *why* the agent thought it was appropriate.
    31→
    32→In a coding domain: the chain records that the agent modified `auth.rs` and added a new endpoint. It does not record which code intelligence signals, which test failures, which PRD requirements, or which Neuro entries informed that implementation choice.
    33→
    34→### Gap 2: No Knowledge Provenance
    35→
    36→The Neuro store (Roko's persistent knowledge system, `roko-neuro`) contains entries such as "high code churn in authentication modules correlates with security vulnerabilities" or "momentum strategies fail in range-bound markets." Which episodes taught these lessons? How many observations support them? There is no way to trace a Neuro entry back to its evidential basis without the DAG.
    37→
    38→### Gap 3: Trust Requires Reputation
    39→
    40→When agents in a Collective (a group of cooperating agents on the Korai network) establish trust, they rely on reputation scores and attestations. Reputation is backward-looking and gameable: an agent can build reputation through conservative behavior, then exploit that trust. **Verifiable reasoning quality** — cryptographic proof that decisions were grounded in real observations — would be a stronger trust signal than reputation alone.
    41→
    42→### Gap 4: Auditing Requires Revelation
    43→
    44→Stakeholders (depositors, operators, regulators) want to audit decision quality. Today this requires revealing the strategy itself. There is no way to prove "my decisions were well-reasoned" without showing the reasoning — unless zero-knowledge proofs are used (see §Zero-Knowledge Proofs below).
    45→
    46→---
    47→
    48→## Mathematical Foundations
    49→
    50→### DAG Structure
    51→
    52→A Witness DAG is a directed acyclic graph **W = (V, E)** where:
    53→
    54→**Vertices V:** Every cognitive event produces a vertex. Five vertex types map to steps of the Universal Cognitive Loop (the 9-step Synapse Loop that every Roko agent runs at its own timescale):
    55→
    56→| Type | Label | Created at Loop Step | Description |
    57→|---|---|---|---|
    58→| Observation | O | Step 1 (PERCEIVE) | Raw perceptual data: price feeds, on-chain events, code intelligence signals, test results, file changes |
    59→| Prediction | P | Step 3 (ATTEND) | Forecasts derived from observations: "this refactoring will break 3 tests" or "ETH will decline 3% in 10 ticks" |
    60→| Decision | D | Steps 4-6 (INTEGRATE, ACT, VERIFY) | Actions chosen based on predictions: "modify auth.rs" or "swap 10 ETH for USDC" |
    61→| Resolution | R | Step 8 (ADAPT) | Observed outcomes: "compilation succeeded, 2 tests failed" or "swap executed at 3,201" |
    62→| NeuroEntry | G | Step 9 (META-COGNIZE) | Learned knowledge: "this pattern of test failure indicates missing error handling" |
    63→
    64→Note: The legacy specification used "GrimoireEntry" for the fifth vertex type. In the current architecture, Grimoire has been renamed to Neuro (`roko-neuro`), so this type is "NeuroEntry" — a learned knowledge entry persisted in the Neuro store.
    65→
    66→**Edges E:** Directed edges encode "was used to produce." Direction points from input to output:
    67→
    68→- **O → P**: "Observation O was used to generate prediction P."
    69→- **P → D**: "Prediction P informed decision D."
    70→- **D → R**: "Decision D produced resolution R."
    71→- **P → G**: "Prediction P contributed to Neuro entry G."
    72→- **R → G**: "Resolution R contributed to Neuro entry G."
    73→- **G → P**: "Neuro entry G influenced prediction P." (Knowledge feedback loop.)
    74→
    75→### Cryptographic Commitment
    76→
    77→Each vertex carries two hashes:
    78→
    79→**Content hash** — commits to the vertex's data. Two vertices with identical content produce identical content hashes:
    80→
    81→```
    82→h(v) = BLAKE3(type || timestamp || content(v))
    83→```
    84→
    85→**Commitment hash** — commits to both the vertex's content and its entire ancestry. This is the Merkle property: modifying any vertex invalidates the commitment hashes of all its descendants:
    86→
    87→```
    88→c(v) = BLAKE3(h(v) || c(parent_1) || c(parent_2) || ... || c(parent_n))
    89→```
    90→
    91→Parent commitment hashes are sorted lexicographically before hashing to ensure deterministic commitment regardless of edge insertion order.
    92→
    93→**Why BLAKE3:** BLAKE3 is chosen over SHA-256 for witness hashing: 3-5x faster on modern hardware, tree-based structure enables incremental hashing of event streams, and 256-bit output provides equivalent collision resistance. This choice is consistent with the Engram's `ContentHash` which also uses BLAKE3 (see the Engram struct in `roko-core`).
    94→
    95→### Tamper Evidence
    96→
    97→The commitment hash `c(v)` of any vertex commits to the entire subgraph that produced it. If an attacker modifies observation O_17 that was used to generate prediction P_8, then:
    98→
    99→1. `c(O_17)` changes because the content changed
   100→2. `c(P_8)` changes because it includes `c(O_17)` as a parent

1→# Adaptive Risk Management: Five-Layer Runtime Risk Control
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L3 Harness (runtime risk assessment), Cross-cut (Daimon motivation)
     4→>
     5→> **Crate**: Target: `roko-agent` (risk engine), `roko-daimon` (behavioral state modulation)
     6→>
     7→> **Synapse traits**: `Scorer` (rate risk), `Gate` (enforce risk limits), `Policy` (adapt guardrails)
     8→>
     9→> **Prerequisites**: [00-defense-in-depth.md](00-defense-in-depth.md), [08-threat-model.md](08-threat-model.md)
    10→
    11→
    12→> **Implementation**: Specified
    13→
    14→---
    15→
    16→## Overview
    17→
    18→The adaptive risk system provides five layers of runtime risk control that self-evolve within hard bounds. Every layer operates as T0 (deterministic Rust, no LLM calls, zero inference cost per tick). The LLM proposes actions; the risk engine disposes.
    19→
    20→The five layers, ordered by increasing cost:
    21→
    22→| Layer | Name | What It Does | Enforcement Point |
    23→|-------|------|-------------|-------------------|
    24→| 1 | Hard Shields | Immutable constraints (PolicyCage for chain, SafetyLayer for general) | Pre-execution gate |
    25→| 2 | Position Sizing | Kelly-criterion-based allocation with confidence modulation | Pre-execution adjustment |
    26→| 3 | Adaptive Guardrails | Bayesian trust expansion/contraction | Pre-execution gate + post-turn update |
    27→| 4 | Health Observation | Anomaly detection, health scoring | Post-turn monitoring |
    28→| 5 | Domain Threat Detection | Domain-specific threats (MEV for chain, supply chain for code) | Pre- and post-execution |
    29→
    30→---
    31→
    32→## Layer 1: Hard Shields
    33→
    34→Hard shields are immutable constraints that cannot be overridden by the agent, the risk engine, or even the operator without a configuration change and restart.
    35→
    36→### General-Purpose Agents
    37→
    38→For general-purpose agents, hard shields are implemented via the `SafetyLayer` (see [00-defense-in-depth.md](00-defense-in-depth.md)):
    39→
    40→- **BashPolicy**: Deny patterns for dangerous commands (rm -rf, sudo, fork bombs)
    41→- **GitPolicy**: Protected branches (main/master), force-push blocking
    42→- **NetworkPolicy**: HTTPS-only, private network blocking, host allowlists
    43→- **PathPolicy**: Worktree sandboxing, escape prevention
    44→- **RateLimiter**: 60 calls/60s sliding window per (role, tool)
    45→
    46→### Chain-Domain Agents
    47→
    48→For chain-domain agents, hard shields include the PolicyCage smart contract:
    49→
    50→- **Spending limits**: Per-transaction, per-session, per-day caps
    51→- **Asset allowlists**: Only approved tokens and protocols
    52→- **Slippage bounds**: Maximum price impact per trade (enforced on-chain)
    53→- **Circuit breakers**: Automatic pause at configurable drawdown thresholds (13%/7%/3%)
    54→
    55→The PolicyCage is an on-chain smart contract — even if the agent's entire runtime is compromised, the on-chain constraints hold.
    56→
    57→---
    58→
    59→## Layer 2: Position Sizing (Fractional Kelly)
    60→
    61→### Theory
    62→
    63→Kelly's criterion (Kelly, 1956) determines the growth-optimal allocation for a repeated game with known edge and volatility: `f* = edge / variance`. Full Kelly sizing assumes infinite repetition, known edge, and tolerance for 50%+ drawdowns — none of which hold for an autonomous agent.
    64→
    65→Carta et al. (2020) demonstrated via Monte Carlo simulation that full Kelly requires thousands of trades before converging to the theoretical growth rate, and triple Kelly leads to certain ruin. Half-Kelly captures approximately 75% of optimal growth with dramatically reduced drawdown risk.
    66→
    67→Busseti, Ryu, and Boyd (2016) extended this with risk-constrained Kelly gambling formulated as a convex optimization problem that guarantees drawdown probability stays below a specified level.
    68→
    69→### Confidence-Modulated Scaling
    70→
    71→The Kelly fraction scales with operational confidence:
    72→
    73→```
    74→kelly_fraction = base_kelly * confidence_multiplier(operational_confidence)
    75→
    76→At confidence 0.0: ~10% of growth-optimal (maximum caution)
    77→At confidence 0.9: ~50% of growth-optimal (half-Kelly)
    78→```
    79→
    80→The sigmoid confidence multiplier: `f(c) = 0.1 + 0.4 * sigmoid(10 * (c - 0.5))`
    81→
    82→A new agent starts at confidence ~0.25 (weakly pessimistic prior). The sigmoid maps this to a multiplier around 0.11. After hundreds of successful operations, confidence rises toward 0.8-0.9, and the multiplier approaches 0.5. The agent earns the right to larger actions through demonstrated competence, not through time alone.
    83→
    84→### For General-Purpose Agents
    85→
    86→Position sizing for code agents translates to scope sizing:
    87→- How many files can this agent modify in one task?
    88→- How large a refactoring is it trusted to perform?
    89→- How many concurrent worktrees can it manage?
    90→
    91→The same Kelly-inspired framework applies: start conservative (single-file changes), expand with demonstrated competence (multi-file refactors, architectural changes).
    92→
    93→---
    94→
    95→## Layer 3: Bayesian Adaptive Guardrails
    96→
    97→### Operational Confidence (Beta-Binomial Model)
    98→
    99→The core question: how much should the system trust an agent's decisions? Fixed limits are wrong in both directions — too tight and the agent cannot operate, too loose and it can cause damage before earning trust.
   100→
   101→Berkenkamp et al. (2017) formalized safe exploration using Gaussian process models with Lyapunov stability guarantees. Their algorithm safely collects data and gradually expands the safe region. Roko adapts this principle.
   102→
   103→```rust
   104→/// Tracks operational confidence across multiple competence dimensions
   105→/// using Beta-Binomial models with asymmetric learning rates.
   106→pub struct OperationalConfidenceTracker {
   107→    pub dimensions: HashMap<String, BetaDistribution>,
   108→}
   109→
   110→pub struct BetaDistribution {
   111→    pub alpha: f64,  // Success count + prior
   112→    pub beta: f64,   // Failure count + prior
   113→}
   114→```
   115→
   116→**Weakly pessimistic priors**: `alpha=1, beta=3` starts at mean 0.25. The composite confidence uses the geometric mean of lower 95% credible intervals across all dimensions, ensuring a single poorly-calibrated dimension drags everything down.
   117→
   118→**Asymmetric learning**: Failures count 1.5x. In portfolio management and software engineering alike, a single catastrophic failure can wipe out dozens of successes. The 1.5x multiplier is conservative relative to the actual loss asymmetry but aggressive enough to demote underperforming strategies within 10-15 failures.
   119→
   120→### Guardrail Evolution
   121→
   122→Guardrails evolve with confidence, tighten during failure streaks, and contract during high-risk periods:
   123→
   124→```
   125→effective_limit = hard_shield_limit × base_multiplier × context_multiplier
   126→
   127→base_multiplier = 0.2 + 0.8 × confidence   // [20%, 100%] of hard shield
   128→context_multiplier = f(failure_rate, task_complexity, domain_risk)
   129→```
   130→
   131→At low confidence (new agent): effective limits are 20% of hard shields.
   132→At high confidence (proven agent): effective limits approach 100% of hard shields.
   133→
   134→---
   135→
   136→## Layer 4: Health Observation
   137→
   138→### Anomaly Detection
   139→
   140→The observation layer monitors agent behavior for anomalies:
   141→
   142→- **Ghost turn detection**: Turns where the agent produces no meaningful output (empty responses, repeated failures)
   143→- **Efficiency degradation**: Declining tokens-per-successful-outcome ratio
   144→- **Gate pass rate tracking**: EMA of gate pass rates per rung
   145→- **Context attribution**: Whether the agent is using provided context effectively
   146→
   147→### Health Score
   148→
   149→A composite health score feeds into the circuit breaker:
   150→
   151→```
   152→health_score = w1 × gate_pass_rate + w2 × efficiency_trend + w3 × (1 - ghost_turn_rate) + w4 × context_attribution_rate
   153→```
   154→
   155→Default weights: gate_pass_rate 0.4, efficiency_trend 0.2, ghost_turn_rate 0.2, context_attribution 0.2.
   156→
   157→When health score drops below threshold (default 0.3), the conductor's circuit breaker opens.
   158→
   159→---
   160→
   161→## Layer 5: Domain Threat Detection
   162→
   163→### Code Domain
   164→
   165→- **Dangerous import detection**: Checks for imports of known-dangerous modules (os.system, subprocess, eval)
   166→- **Secret detection in output**: ScrubPolicy catches credentials in generated code
   167→- **Dependency confusion**: Checks proposed dependency additions against known-good registries
   168→
   169→### Chain Domain
   170→
   171→- **MEV detection**: Pattern matching for sandwich attacks, front-running, JIT liquidity (see [10-mev-protection.md](10-mev-protection.md))
   172→- **Oracle manipulation detection**: Multi-oracle divergence, TWAP deviation
   173→- **Cross-protocol contagion**: CFI and ASRI metrics (see [08-threat-model.md](08-threat-model.md))
   174→

   176→
   177→## OperationalConfidenceTracker: full implementation
   178→
   179→```rust
   180→use std::collections::HashMap;
   181→
   182→/// Tracks operational confidence across multiple competence dimensions
   183→/// using Beta-Binomial models with asymmetric learning rates.
   184→pub struct OperationalConfidenceTracker {
   185→    /// Per-dimension Beta distributions.
   186→    pub dimensions: HashMap<String, BetaDistribution>,
   187→    /// Failure multiplier for asymmetric learning.
   188→    /// Default: 1.5 (failures count 1.5x).
   189→    pub failure_weight: f64,
   190→}
   191→
   192→/// Beta distribution parameters for a single dimension.
   193→pub struct BetaDistribution {
   194→    /// Success pseudo-count (alpha). Starts at 1.0 (weakly pessimistic).
   195→    pub alpha: f64,
   196→    /// Failure pseudo-count (beta). Starts at 3.0 (weakly pessimistic).
   197→    pub beta: f64,
   198→}
   199→
   200→impl BetaDistribution {
   201→    /// Weakly pessimistic prior: mean = 1/(1+3) = 0.25.
   202→    pub fn pessimistic_prior() -> Self {
   203→        Self { alpha: 1.0, beta: 3.0 }
   204→    }
   205→
   206→    /// Mean of the Beta distribution: alpha / (alpha + beta).
   207→    pub fn mean(&self) -> f64 {
   208→        self.alpha / (self.alpha + self.beta)
   209→    }
   210→
   211→    /// Variance: alpha*beta / ((alpha+beta)^2 * (alpha+beta+1)).
   212→    pub fn variance(&self) -> f64 {
   213→        let sum = self.alpha + self.beta;
   214→        (self.alpha * self.beta) / (sum * sum * (sum + 1.0))
   215→    }
   216→
   217→    /// Lower bound of 95% credible interval.
   218→    /// Uses the normal approximation: mean - 1.96 * sqrt(variance).
   219→    /// For small alpha+beta, this underestimates -- conservative, which is correct.
   220→    pub fn lower_95(&self) -> f64 {
   221→        (self.mean() - 1.96 * self.variance().sqrt()).max(0.0)
   222→    }
   223→
   224→    /// Record a success: increment alpha by 1.
   225→    pub fn record_success(&mut self) {
   226→        self.alpha += 1.0;
   227→    }
   228→
   229→    /// Record a failure: increment beta by failure_weight.
   230→    pub fn record_failure(&mut self, weight: f64) {
   231→        self.beta += weight;
   232→    }
   233→}
   234→
   235→impl OperationalConfidenceTracker {
   236→    pub fn new() -> Self {
   237→        Self {
   238→            dimensions: HashMap::new(),
   239→            failure_weight: 1.5,
   240→        }
   241→    }
   242→
   243→    /// Register a competence dimension with a pessimistic prior.
   244→    /// Standard dimensions: "gate_pass", "tool_success", "cost_efficiency",
   245→    /// "context_utilization", "task_completion".
   246→    pub fn register_dimension(&mut self, name: &str) {
   247→        self.dimensions
   248→            .entry(name.to_string())
   249→            .or_insert_with(BetaDistribution::pessimistic_prior);
   250→    }
   251→
   252→    /// Record a success in a dimension.
   253→    pub fn record_success(&mut self, dimension: &str) {
   254→        if let Some(dist) = self.dimensions.get_mut(dimension) {
   255→            dist.record_success();
   256→        }
   257→    }
   258→
   259→    /// Record a failure in a dimension.
   260→    /// Failures are weighted by self.failure_weight (default 1.5x).
   261→    pub fn record_failure(&mut self, dimension: &str) {
   262→        let w = self.failure_weight;
   263→        if let Some(dist) = self.dimensions.get_mut(dimension) {
   264→            dist.record_failure(w);
   265→        }
   266→    }
   267→
   268→    /// Composite confidence: geometric mean of lower 95% credible intervals.
   269→    /// The geometric mean ensures a single poorly-calibrated dimension
   270→    /// drags everything down.
   271→    pub fn composite_confidence(&self) -> f64 {
   272→        if self.dimensions.is_empty() {
   273→            return 0.0;
   274→        }
   275→        let product: f64 = self
   276→            .dimensions
   277→            .values()
   278→            .map(|d| d.lower_95().max(0.001)) // floor to avoid zero-product
   279→            .product();
   280→        product.powf(1.0 / self.dimensions.len() as f64)
   281→    }
   282→}
   283→```
   284→
   285→### confidence_multiplier() and effective_limit()
   286→
   287→```rust
   288→/// Sigmoid confidence multiplier.
   289→/// Maps confidence [0, 1] to Kelly fraction multiplier [0.1, 0.5].
   290→///
   291→/// At confidence 0.0: returns ~0.1 (10% of growth-optimal).
   292→/// At confidence 0.5: returns ~0.3 (inflection point).
   293→/// At confidence 1.0: returns ~0.5 (half-Kelly).
   294→pub fn confidence_multiplier(confidence: f64) -> f64 {
   295→    let sigmoid = 1.0 / (1.0 + (-10.0 * (confidence - 0.5)).exp());
   296→    0.1 + 0.4 * sigmoid
   297→}
   298→
   299→/// Compute effective limit for a given action.
   300→///
   301→/// effective_limit = hard_shield_limit * base_multiplier * context_multiplier
   302→///
   303→/// base_multiplier = 0.2 + 0.8 * confidence  (range: [0.2, 1.0])
   304→/// context_multiplier = f(failure_rate, task_complexity, domain_risk)
   305→pub fn effective_limit(
   306→    hard_shield_limit: f64,
   307→    confidence: f64,
   308→    failure_rate: f64,
   309→    task_complexity: f64,
   310→    domain_risk: f64,
   311→) -> f64 {
   312→    let base_multiplier = 0.2 + 0.8 * confidence;
   313→
   314→    // Context multiplier: penalize recent failures, complex tasks, risky domains.
   315→    // Each factor in [0, 1]; product gives the combined discount.
   316→    let failure_factor = 1.0 - (failure_rate * 0.5).min(0.8);   // max 80% reduction
   317→    let complexity_factor = 1.0 - (task_complexity * 0.3).min(0.6); // max 60% reduction
   318→    let risk_factor = 1.0 - (domain_risk * 0.4).min(0.7);       // max 70% reduction
   319→    let context_multiplier = failure_factor * complexity_factor * risk_factor;
   320→
   321→    hard_shield_limit * base_multiplier * context_multiplier
   322→}
   323→```
   324→
   325→### Risk-constrained Kelly optimization (Busseti et al. 2016)
   326→
   327→The standard Kelly criterion maximizes log-growth: `f* = edge / variance`. Busseti, Ryu, and Boyd reformulate this as a convex optimization problem with an explicit drawdown constraint:
   328→
   329→```
   330→maximize    E[log(1 + f * X)]
   331→subject to  P(drawdown > d) <= epsilon
   332→            0 <= f <= f_max
   333→```
   334→
   335→Where `X` is the random return, `d` is the maximum acceptable drawdown, and `epsilon` is the probability bound on exceeding that drawdown.
   336→
   337→Algorithm (pseudocode):
   338→
   339→```
   340→risk_constrained_kelly(edge, variance, max_drawdown, epsilon):
   341→    # Step 1: Compute unconstrained Kelly fraction.
   342→    f_kelly = edge / variance
   343→
   344→    # Step 2: Compute drawdown-constrained upper bound.
   345→    # From Busseti et al., the bound on ruin probability
   346→    # for fractional Kelly is approximately:
   347→    #   P(drawdown > d) ≈ exp(-2 * d * (1 - f/f_kelly) / (f^2 * variance))
   348→    # Solving for f given P = epsilon:
   349→    #   f_dd = solve: exp(-2 * d * (1 - f/f_kelly) / (f^2 * variance)) = epsilon
   350→
   351→    # Step 3: Iterative bisection (convex, so bisection converges).
   352→    f_low = 0.0
   353→    f_high = f_kelly
   354→    for _ in 0..50:
   355→        f_mid = (f_low + f_high) / 2
   356→        ruin_prob = exp(-2 * max_drawdown * (1 - f_mid / f_kelly) / (f_mid^2 * variance))
   357→        if ruin_prob > epsilon:
   358→            f_high = f_mid   # Too aggressive
   359→        else:
   360→            f_low = f_mid    # Can afford more
   361→
   362→    # Step 4: Apply confidence modulation.
   363→    f_constrained = f_low
   364→    return f_constrained * confidence_multiplier(operational_confidence)
   365→```
   366→
   367→**Configuration parameters:**
   368→
   369→```toml
   370→[agent.risk.kelly]
   371→base_kelly_fraction = 0.5     # Starting Kelly fraction before modulation. Range: 0.1..1.0.
   372→max_drawdown = 0.13           # Maximum acceptable drawdown. Range: 0.01..0.5.
   373→drawdown_epsilon = 0.05       # Probability bound on exceeding drawdown. Range: 0.001..0.2.
   374→confidence_floor = 0.1        # Minimum confidence multiplier. Range: 0.05..0.3.
   375→confidence_ceiling = 0.5      # Maximum confidence multiplier (half-Kelly). Range: 0.3..1.0.
   376→```
   377→
   378→For code-domain agents, Kelly sizing translates to scope limits:
   379→
   380→| Confidence range | Scope allowed | Files per task | Refactor depth |
   381→|-----------------|---------------|----------------|----------------|
   382→| 0.0 - 0.3 | Single-file, single-function | 1 | Leaf functions only |
   383→| 0.3 - 0.6 | Single-file, multi-function | 1-3 | Module-level |
   384→| 0.6 - 0.8 | Multi-file, single crate | 3-10 | Cross-module |
   385→| 0.8 - 1.0 | Multi-crate refactoring | 10+ | Architectural |
   386→
   387→### Confidence evolution state machine
   388→
   389→```
   390→  +------------+     success    +------------+     success    +-----------+
   391→  |  Cautious  | ------------> | Developing | ------------> |  Capable  |
   392→  | conf<0.3   |               | 0.3<=c<0.7 |               | c>=0.7    |
   393→  +-----+------+               +-----+------+               +-----+-----+
   394→        ^                            |                             |
   395→        |     failure streak         |  failure streak             |
   396→        |     (>=5 consecutive)      |  (>=3 consecutive)         |
   397→        +----------------------------+-----------------------------+
   398→                                     |
   399→                                     v
   400→                              +------+------+
   401→                              |  Recovery   |
   402→                              | temp tighten|
   403→                              +------+------+
   404→                                     |
   405→                                     | 10 turns without failure
   406→                                     v
   407→                              (return to previous state)
   408→```
   409→
   410→| From | To | Trigger |
   411→|------|----|---------|
   412→| Cautious | Developing | composite_confidence crosses 0.3 upward |
   413→| Developing | Capable | composite_confidence crosses 0.7 upward |
   414→| Capable | Recovery | 3+ consecutive failures in any dimension |
   415→| Developing | Recovery | 5+ consecutive failures in any dimension |
   416→| Recovery | previous state | 10 consecutive turns without failure |
   417→| Any | Cautious | composite_confidence drops below 0.3 |
   418→

   420→
   421→## Integration with Daimon
   422→
   423→The Daimon (motivation/affect engine) modulates risk tolerance based on the agent's behavioral state:
   424→
   425→| Daimon State | Risk Tolerance Modifier | Rationale |
   426→|-------------|------------------------|-----------|
   427→| Engaged | 1.0× (normal) | Agent performing well, standard limits |
   428→| Struggling | 0.6× (tightened) | Reduce scope to help agent recover |
   429→| Coasting | 0.8× (slightly tightened) | Agent may be cutting corners |
   430→| Exploring | 1.2× (loosened) | Exploration needs room for failure |
   431→| Focused | 1.0× (normal) | Concentrated work, standard limits |
   432→| Resting | 0.3× (heavily tightened) | Minimal operations during rest |
   433→
   434→The PAD (Pleasure-Arousal-Dominance) vector from the Daimon modulates the effective confidence used for guardrail evolution:
   435→
   436→```
   437→effective_confidence = base_confidence × daimon_modifier(pad_vector)
   438→```
   439→
   440→### Daimon state to risk_tolerance_modifier mapping
   441→
   442→```rust
   443→/// Compute risk tolerance modifier from Daimon behavioral state.
   444→/// Returns a multiplier applied to effective_confidence.
   445→pub fn risk_tolerance_modifier(state: &DaimonState) -> f64 {
   446→    match state {
   447→        DaimonState::Engaged => 1.0,     // Normal operation.
   448→        DaimonState::Struggling => 0.6,  // Tighten limits to help recovery.
   449→        DaimonState::Coasting => 0.8,    // Slight tightening; may be cutting corners.
   450→        DaimonState::Exploring => 1.2,   // Exploration needs room for failure.
   451→        DaimonState::Focused => 1.0,     // Concentrated work, standard limits.
   452→        DaimonState::Resting => 0.3,     // Minimal operations during rest.
   453→    }
   454→}
   455→
   456→/// Full effective confidence computation including Daimon modulation.
   457→pub fn daimon_adjusted_confidence(
   458→    tracker: &OperationalConfidenceTracker,
   459→    daimon_state: &DaimonState,
   460→) -> f64 {
   461→    let base = tracker.composite_confidence();
   462→    let modifier = risk_tolerance_modifier(daimon_state);
   463→    (base * modifier).clamp(0.0, 1.0)
   464→}
   465→```
   466→
   467→### roko.toml kelly_fraction configuration
   468→
   469→```toml
   470→[agent.risk]
   471→# Base Kelly fraction before confidence modulation.
   472→# Half-Kelly (0.5) captures ~75% of optimal growth with much lower drawdown.
   473→# Range: 0.1..1.0. Default: 0.5.
   474→kelly_fraction = 0.5
   475→
   476→# Hard shield limits (immutable per-domain caps).
   477→max_files_per_task = 20          # Code domain. Range: 1..100.
   478→max_concurrent_worktrees = 3     # Code domain. Range: 1..10.
   479→max_transaction_value_eth = 1.0  # Chain domain. Range: 0.001..100.0.
   480→max_daily_spend_usd = 100.0     # Cost domain. Range: 1.0..10000.0.
   481→
   482→# Adaptive guardrail parameters.
   483→confidence_floor = 0.1           # Minimum multiplier (never below 10% of hard shield).
   484→failure_weight = 1.5             # Failures count 1.5x in Beta update. Range: 1.0..3.0.
   485→recovery_window_turns = 10       # Turns without failure to exit Recovery state.
   486→```
   487→
   488→### Integration wiring path
   489→
   490→```
   491→orchestrate.rs: PlanRunner::run_task()
   492→  |
   493→  +--> OperationalConfidenceTracker::composite_confidence()
   494→  |      reads from: .roko/learn/gate-thresholds.json (EMA per rung)
   495→  |
   496→  +--> daimon_adjusted_confidence()
   497→  |      reads from: Daimon PAD vector (roko-daimon)
   498→  |
   499→  +--> confidence_multiplier() --> kelly_fraction
   500→  |
   501→  +--> effective_limit()
   502→  |      applies: hard_shield * base_mult * context_mult
   503→  |
   504→  +--> SafetyLayer::check_pre_execution()
   505→         enforces: effective limits per action
   506→```
   507→
   508→### Test criteria
   509→
   510→- `BetaDistribution::pessimistic_prior()` starts at mean 0.25 (alpha=1, beta=3)
   511→- `record_failure()` increments beta by `failure_weight`, not by 1.0
   512→- `composite_confidence()` uses geometric mean: one dimension at 0.01 drags composite below 0.1
   513→- `confidence_multiplier(0.0)` returns approximately 0.1; `confidence_multiplier(1.0)` returns approximately 0.5
   514→- `effective_limit()` at confidence 0.0 returns 20% of hard shield
   515→- `effective_limit()` at confidence 1.0 with zero failure/complexity/risk returns 100% of hard shield
   516→- `risk_tolerance_modifier` for Resting state (0.3) combined with low confidence (0.2) produces a heavily constrained limit
   517→- Kelly fraction stays within `[confidence_floor, confidence_ceiling]` for all inputs
   518→- Recovery state exits after `recovery_window_turns` consecutive successes
   519→

   526→| Kelly (1956) | Growth-optimal allocation criterion |
   527→| Carta et al. (2020) | Monte Carlo analysis of Kelly variants |
   528→| Busseti, Ryu, Boyd (2016) | Risk-constrained Kelly gambling |
   529→| MacLean, Ziemba, Blazenko (1992) | Growth versus security in Kelly betting |
   530→| Berkenkamp et al. (2017) | Safe exploration with Gaussian process models |
   531→| Milionis, Moallemi, Roughgarden, Zhang (2022) | LVR — Loss-Versus-Rebalancing for LP sizing |
   532→| Loesch et al. (2021) | Empirical LP losses across 17 Uniswap v3 pools |
   533→| NASA UAS Risk Assessment | Risk budgets for autonomous aircraft — safety margins per maneuver |
   534→| FAA AI Safety Assurance Roadmap (2025) | Phased AI integration with safety budget tiers |
   535→| CSA Securing Agentic Control Plane (2026) | Kill switches and blast-radius limits as first-class primitives |
   536→| Agent-SafetyBench (arXiv:2412.14470, 2024) | 349 environments, 2000 tests — no agent scored >60% safety compliance |
   537→
   538→---
   539→
   540→## Safety Budgets: Risk Allocation Framework
   541→
   542→A safety budget is a hard limit on how much uncertainty, autonomy, and irreversibility an agent is permitted to consume before requiring human review or triggering a halt. Inspired by NASA UAS risk budgets and FAA AI Safety Assurance Roadmap (2025).
   543→
   544→### Budget Dimensions
   545→
   546→Define five orthogonal budget dimensions:
   547→
   548→```rust
   549→/// Safety budget: hard limits on agent risk consumption per session.
   550→/// Each dimension tracks a different axis of risk.
   551→///
   552→/// Inspired by NASA UAS risk budgets and CSA agentic safety
   553→/// recommendations (2026).
   554→pub struct SafetyBudget {
   555→    /// Irreversibility budget: sum of irreversibility scores.
   556→    /// Read operations score 0.0; permanent deletions score 1.0.
   557→    /// Range for limit: 0.1..100.0. Default: 10.0.
   558→    pub irreversibility_limit: f64,
   559→
   560→    /// Blast radius budget: maximum files modified per session.
   561→    /// Prevents a single agent run from touching too much code.
   562→    /// Range: 1..1000. Default: 50.
   563→    pub blast_radius_file_limit: usize,
   564→
   565→    /// Footprint budget: maximum external interactions.
   566→    /// Counts tool calls, API requests, process spawns.
   567→    /// Range: 10..10000. Default: 500.
   568→    pub footprint_limit: usize,
   569→
   570→    /// Uncertainty budget: tokens for low-confidence decisions.
   571→    /// Each decision with confidence < uncertainty_threshold consumes one token.
   572→    /// When exhausted, agent must escalate to human.
   573→    /// Range: 1..100. Default: 10.
   574→    pub uncertainty_tokens: usize,
   575→
   576→    /// Cost budget: maximum spend (USD) on inference + tools.
   577→    /// Range: 0.01..10000.0. Default: 50.0.
   578→    pub cost_limit_usd: f64,
   579→}
   580→
   581→/// Tracks budget consumption during a session.
   582→pub struct SafetyBudgetTracker {
   583→    pub budget: SafetyBudget,
   584→    pub usage: SafetyBudgetUsage,
   585→    /// Threshold below which a decision is "uncertain".
   586→    pub uncertainty_threshold: f64,
   587→}
   588→
   589→pub struct SafetyBudgetUsage {
   590→    pub irreversibility_consumed: f64,
   591→    pub files_touched: HashSet<String>,
   592→    pub footprint_count: usize,
   593→    pub uncertainty_tokens_used: usize,
   594→    pub cost_consumed_usd: f64,
   595→}
   596→
   597→impl SafetyBudgetTracker {
   598→    /// Check whether a proposed action is within budget.
   599→    /// Returns the limiting dimension if budget would be exceeded.
   600→    pub fn check(&self, action: &ProposedAction) -> BudgetCheckResult {
   601→        let usage = &self.usage;
   602→        let budget = &self.budget;
   603→
   604→        if usage.irreversibility_consumed + action.irreversibility_score
   605→            > budget.irreversibility_limit
   606→        {
   607→            return BudgetCheckResult::Exceeded(BudgetDimension::Irreversibility);
   608→        }
   609→
   610→        let new_file_count = {
   611→            let mut files = usage.files_touched.clone();
   612→            files.extend(action.files_modified.iter().cloned());
   613→            files.len()
   614→        };
   615→        if new_file_count > budget.blast_radius_file_limit {
   616→            return BudgetCheckResult::Exceeded(BudgetDimension::BlastRadius);
   617→        }
   618→
   619→        if usage.footprint_count + action.tool_calls > budget.footprint_limit {
   620→            return BudgetCheckResult::Exceeded(BudgetDimension::Footprint);
   621→        }
   622→
   623→        if action.confidence < self.uncertainty_threshold
   624→            && usage.uncertainty_tokens_used >= budget.uncertainty_tokens
   625→        {
   626→            return BudgetCheckResult::Exceeded(BudgetDimension::Uncertainty);
   627→        }
   628→
   629→        if usage.cost_consumed_usd + action.estimated_cost > budget.cost_limit_usd {
   630→            return BudgetCheckResult::Exceeded(BudgetDimension::Cost);
   631→        }
   632→
   633→        BudgetCheckResult::WithinBudget
   634→    }
   635→
   636→    /// Record an action's consumption against the budget.
   637→    pub fn consume(&mut self, action: &CompletedAction) {
   638→        self.usage.irreversibility_consumed += action.irreversibility_score;
   639→        self.usage.files_touched.extend(action.files_modified.iter().cloned());
   640→        self.usage.footprint_count += action.tool_calls;
   641→        if action.confidence < self.uncertainty_threshold {
   642→            self.usage.uncertainty_tokens_used += 1;
   643→        }
   644→        self.usage.cost_consumed_usd += action.actual_cost;
   645→    }
   646→}
   647→
   648→pub enum BudgetCheckResult {
   649→    WithinBudget,
   650→    Exceeded(BudgetDimension),
   651→}
   652→
   653→pub enum BudgetDimension {
   654→    Irreversibility,
   655→    BlastRadius,
   656→    Footprint,
   657→    Uncertainty,
   658→    Cost,
   659→}
   660→```
   661→
   662→### Irreversibility Scoring
   663→
   664→Actions are scored on a 0.0-1.0 irreversibility scale:
   665→
   666→| Action | Score | Rationale |
   667→|--------|-------|-----------|
   668→| read_file, glob, grep | 0.0 | Pure observation, no side effects |
   669→| write_file (new file) | 0.2 | Reversible: delete the file |
   670→| edit_file | 0.3 | Reversible via git: but requires manual intervention |
   671→| bash (read-only: ls, cat) | 0.0 | No side effects |
   672→| bash (build: cargo build) | 0.1 | Artifacts can be cleaned |
   673→| bash (destructive: rm) | 0.8 | Hard to reverse without backup |
   674→| git commit | 0.3 | Reversible via revert |
   675→| git push | 0.6 | Affects shared state, requires force-push to undo |
   676→| Network API call (GET) | 0.0 | Read-only |
   677→| Network API call (POST) | 0.5 | May have irreversible side effects |
   678→
   679→```rust
   680→/// Compute irreversibility score for a tool call.
   681→pub fn irreversibility_score(tool: &str, args: &serde_json::Value) -> f64 {
   682→    match tool {
   683→        "read_file" | "glob" | "grep" => 0.0,
   684→        "write_file" => 0.2,
   685→        "edit_file" => 0.3,
   686→        "bash" => score_bash_irreversibility(args),
   687→        "git_commit" => 0.3,
   688→        "git_push" => 0.6,
   689→        "web_fetch" => 0.0,
   690→        "web_search" => 0.0,
   691→        _ => 0.5, // Unknown tools get moderate score
   692→    }
   693→}
   694→
   695→fn score_bash_irreversibility(args: &serde_json::Value) -> f64 {
   696→    let command = args.get("command")
   697→        .and_then(|v| v.as_str())
   698→        .unwrap_or("");
   699→    if command.starts_with("ls") || command.starts_with("cat")
   700→        || command.starts_with("echo") || command.starts_with("grep") {
   701→        0.0
   702→    } else if command.contains("rm ") || command.contains("rmdir") {
   703→        0.8
   704→    } else if command.starts_with("cargo build") || command.starts_with("cargo test") {
   705→        0.1
   706→    } else if command.starts_with("cargo publish") {
   707→        0.9
   708→    } else {
   709→        0.4 // Unknown bash commands get moderate score
   710→    }
   711→}
   712→```
   713→
   714→### Budget Tiers
   715→
   716→Different task types receive different budgets:
   717→
   718→| Task Type | Irreversibility | Files | Footprint | Uncertainty | Cost |
   719→|-----------|----------------|-------|-----------|-------------|------|
   720→| Read-only audit | 0.0 | 0 | 100 | 5 | $5 |
   721→| Single-file fix | 3.0 | 3 | 200 | 5 | $10 |
   722→| Multi-file implementation | 15.0 | 20 | 500 | 10 | $50 |
   723→| Architectural refactor | 50.0 | 100 | 2000 | 20 | $200 |
   724→| Full plan execution | 100.0 | 500 | 5000 | 50 | $500 |
   725→
   726→### Integration with Adaptive Risk
   727→
   728→Safety budgets compose with the existing five-layer adaptive risk system:
   729→
   730→```
   731→effective_budget = base_budget * confidence_multiplier * daimon_modifier
   732→
   733→Where:
   734→  base_budget = task-type default from the tier table
   735→  confidence_multiplier = from OperationalConfidenceTracker (Layer 3)
   736→  daimon_modifier = from Daimon behavioral state (0.3 for Resting, 1.2 for Exploring)
   737→```
   738→
   739→A new agent (low confidence, 0.2 multiplier) working on a multi-file task gets:
   740→- Irreversibility: 15.0 x 0.2 = 3.0 (equivalent to a single-file fix)
   741→- Files: 20 x 0.2 = 4
   742→- Footprint: 500 x 0.2 = 100
   743→
   744→### Budget Exhaustion Response
   745→
   746→When any budget dimension is exhausted:
   747→
   748→```rust
   749→/// Response to budget exhaustion.
   750→pub fn on_budget_exhausted(
   751→    dimension: BudgetDimension,
   752→    usage: &SafetyBudgetUsage,
   753→    budget: &SafetyBudget,
   754→) -> CognitiveSignal {
   755→    match dimension {
   756→        BudgetDimension::Irreversibility => CognitiveSignal::Pause,
   757→        BudgetDimension::BlastRadius => CognitiveSignal::Pause,
   758→        BudgetDimension::Footprint => CognitiveSignal::Cooldown,
   759→        BudgetDimension::Uncertainty => CognitiveSignal::Escalate,
   760→        BudgetDimension::Cost => CognitiveSignal::Shutdown,
   761→    }
   762→}
   763→```
   764→
   765→### Configuration
   766→
   767→```toml
   768→[agent.risk.budget]
   769→# Enable safety budgets. Default: true.
   770→enabled = true
   771→# Default task type (determines base budget tier).
   772→default_task_type = "multi_file"   # "read_only" | "single_file" | "multi_file" | "refactor" | "plan_execution"
   773→# Uncertainty threshold: decisions below this confidence consume uncertainty tokens.
   774→uncertainty_threshold = 0.5    # Range: 0.1..0.9.
   775→# Whether budget scales with confidence. Default: true.
   776→confidence_scaling = true
   777→
   778→[agent.risk.budget.overrides]
   779→# Per-dimension overrides (override the tier defaults).
   780→# irreversibility_limit = 20.0
   781→# blast_radius_file_limit = 30
   782→# footprint_limit = 1000
   783→# uncertainty_tokens = 15
   784→# cost_limit_usd = 100.0
   785→```
   786→
   787→### Test Criteria
   788→
   789→- SafetyBudgetTracker::check() returns WithinBudget for a read-only action with full budget
   790→- SafetyBudgetTracker::check() returns Exceeded(Irreversibility) when irreversibility_consumed + action > limit
   791→- SafetyBudgetTracker::check() returns Exceeded(Uncertainty) when tokens exhausted and confidence < threshold
   792→- SafetyBudgetTracker::consume() correctly increments all usage counters
   793→- irreversibility_score("read_file", _) returns 0.0
   794→- irreversibility_score("bash", rm_command) returns 0.8
   795→- Budget tier "read_only" has 0.0 irreversibility limit (no writes allowed)
   796→- confidence_scaling correctly reduces budget for low-confidence agents
   797→- on_budget_exhausted returns Escalate for Uncertainty (to involve human)
   798→- Budget composition: effective_budget = base x confidence x daimon correctly computes for all states
   799→
   800→---
   801→
   802→## Related Topics
   803→
   804→- [00-defense-in-depth.md](00-defense-in-depth.md) — Hard shields (Layer 1)
   805→- [05-loop-detection.md](05-loop-detection.md) — Circuit breaker and loop defense
   806→- [08-threat-model.md](08-threat-model.md) — Threat-to-layer mapping
   807→- [10-mev-protection.md](10-mev-protection.md) — Layer 5 chain-domain threats
   808→

1→# Cognitive Kernel Primitives: Safety Implications
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L0 Runtime (kernel primitives), L1 Framework (capability enforcement), L3 Harness (signal-based intervention)
     4→>
     5→> **Crate**: Cross-cutting: `roko-runtime` (scheduling, signals), `roko-core` (namespaces, syscalls), `roko-agent` (enforcement)
     6→>
     7→> **Synapse traits**: `Policy` (syscall enforcement), `Substrate` (namespace isolation), `Gate` (signal-driven verification)
     8→>
     9→> **Prerequisites**: [00-defense-in-depth.md](00-defense-in-depth.md), [04-permits-allowlists.md](04-permits-allowlists.md), [06-sandboxing.md](06-sandboxing.md)
    10→
    11→
    12→> **Implementation**: Specified
    13→
    14→---
    15→
    16→## Overview
    17→
    18→Roko implements OS-level primitives for agents that no other agent framework provides. These primitives — Cognitive Namespaces, Cognitive Signals, Cognitive Scheduling, and Engram Syscalls — are inspired by what Linux got right for process management and adapted for cognitive agent management.
    19→
    20→This document covers the **safety implications** of each primitive: how namespaces prevent knowledge leakage, how signals enable safe intervention, how scheduling prevents resource starvation, and how syscalls provide a single enforcement point for all agent actions.
    21→
    22→The key insight: just as a Unix kernel mediates all hardware access through system calls, Roko mediates all cognitive actions through Engram Syscalls. Every file write, API call, knowledge posting, and tool invocation passes through a controlled interface where security, auditing, rate limiting, and cost tracking are enforced uniformly.
    23→
    24→---
    25→
    26→## Cognitive Namespaces
    27→
    28→### What They Are
    29→
    30→Cognitive Namespaces are isolated knowledge spaces with explicit, auditable cross-namespace channels. They are the knowledge-domain equivalent of Linux namespaces (which isolate processes' view of the system into separate PID, network, mount, and user namespaces).
    31→
    32→```rust
    33→/// A cognitive namespace: an isolated knowledge domain.
    34→pub struct CognitiveNamespace {
    35→    /// Unique identifier for this namespace.
    36→    pub id: NamespaceId,
    37→    /// The Substrate (storage) backing this namespace.
    38→    /// Each namespace has its own Substrate instance.
    39→    pub substrate: Arc<dyn Substrate>,
    40→    /// Access control list: who can read/write.
    41→    pub acl: AccessControlList,
    42→    /// Explicit cross-namespace channels.
    43→    /// Knowledge only flows between namespaces through these.
    44→    pub channels: Vec<NamespaceChannel>,
    45→}
    46→
    47→/// A channel between two namespaces with explicit transfer rules.
    48→pub struct NamespaceChannel {
    49→    /// Source namespace.
    50→    pub from: NamespaceId,
    51→    /// Destination namespace.
    52→    pub to: NamespaceId,
    53→    /// Filter: which Engram kinds can flow through.
    54→    pub allowed_kinds: Vec<Kind>,
    55→    /// Whether transfers are logged to the audit chain.
    56→    pub audit_transfers: bool,
    57→    /// Maximum transfer rate (Engrams per second).
    58→    pub rate_limit: Option<f64>,
    59→}
    60→
    61→/// Access control for a namespace.
    62→pub struct AccessControlList {
    63→    /// Agent roles that can read from this namespace.
    64→    pub readers: Vec<AgentRole>,
    65→    /// Agent roles that can write to this namespace.
    66→    pub writers: Vec<AgentRole>,
    67→    /// Whether anonymous (unauthenticated) reads are allowed.
    68→    pub allow_anonymous_read: bool,
    69→}
    70→```
    71→
    72→### Safety Properties
    73→
    74→**Isolation guarantee.** An agent's private knowledge is isolated within its namespace. No other agent can access it without an explicit channel. This prevents:
    75→- Knowledge poisoning: a compromised agent cannot directly corrupt another agent's Neuro store
    76→- Information leakage: proprietary strategies stay within their namespace
    77→- Cross-contamination: experimental knowledge (Dreams output, hypothesis fragments) cannot accidentally pollute production knowledge
    78→
    79→**Explicit channels.** Knowledge sharing happens only through declared channels that log every transfer. This provides:
    80→- Audit trail: every cross-namespace knowledge transfer is recorded with timestamp, source, destination, and content hash
